docs.title: UIAO Documentation Site
docs.text:
Welcome to the Unified Integration Architecture Operations (UIAO) documentation portal.

Start Here -- Canon Overview

New to UIAO? Start with the Canon Overview -- the authoritative entry point for the entire governance corpus. It explains the structure, authority model, and how everything connects.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Resource Description Canon Overview Start here -- governance structure and authority model Corpus Status Dashboard Document maturity and governance health at a glance Document Library Browse all UIAO documents with status badges ADR Index Architecture decisions by theme and lifecycle state Contributor Guide How to add or update documentation Request Export Request PDF or Word exports of any document

docs.title: Operating Environment
docs.text: This site is built with MkDocs Material and deployed via GitHub Pages from the uiao-docs repository.

docs.title: Site Conventions
docs.text:
• Document Identifiers follow the UIAO_<AppendixLetter>_<NN> pattern (e.g., UIAO_A_01)
  • Status Badges indicate document maturity: Current, Draft, Needs Replacing, Needs Creating, Deprecated
  • ADR lifecycle: PROPOSED -> ACCEPTED -> SUPERSEDED | DEPRECATED
  • Dark Mode is available via the toggle in the header
  • Search covers all content including ADRs, appendices, and canon documents

docs.title: Governance Canon
docs.text:
The Canon is organized into five appendices plus the canonical rules, glossary, and ADR corpus:

Appendix Domain Key Topics A -- Adapter Plane Integration adapters Lifecycle, sandbox, hot-swap, health B -- Truth Fabric Canonical state Claim schema, identity anchoring, multi-cloud C -- Drift Fabric Drift detection Detection algorithm, taxonomy, containment D -- Evidence Fabric Audit and compliance Determinism, lifecycle, signing, correlation E -- Governance Plane Governance process ARB, mission corridors, cross-fabric consistency

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: This guide brings a new maintainer from zero context to fully capable of operating and evolving the UIAO SCuBA pipeline.

docs.title: 2. What You Are Responsible For
docs.text:
As a SCuBA maintainer, you:

  • Run SCuBA assessments via the UIAO pipeline
  • Ensure normalization and KSI evaluation succeed
  • Maintain scripts, schemas, and mappings in uiao-core
  • Maintain human-facing reports and docs in uiao-docs
  • Preserve provenance and governance boundaries

docs.title: 3. Documents You Must Read First
docs.text:
1. UIAO SCuBA Pipeline Runbook docs/SCuBA-Pipeline-Runbook.md

  2. Appendix B — UIAO SCuBA Pipeline (Summary & Diagrams) appendices/Appendix-B-UIAO-SCuBA-Pipeline.md

  3. KSI Ruleset and Mapping Tables

  4. KSI rules: uiao-core/ksi/rules/
  5. Mapping tables: Appendix C

docs.title: 4. One-Time Local Setup
docs.text:
• Install PowerShell 7+
  • Install and configure ScubaGear
  • Clone both repos:
  • git clone https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao-core.git
  • git clone https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao-docs.git
  • Ensure you can run PowerShell scripts and commit/push to both repos

docs.title: 5. Running the Pipeline (Day-to-Day)
docs.text:
From the root of uiao-core:

pwsh ./uiao-core/adapters/scuba/run/adapter-run-scuba.ps1 `
     -OutputDirectory ./uiao-core/artifacts/scuba

After a successful run, verify:

  • Raw SCuBA output in artifacts/scuba/raw/
  • Normalized JSON in artifacts/scuba/normalized/
  • KSI report in artifacts/scuba/reports/
  • Provenance manifest in provenance/manifests/

docs.title: 6. Updating the Human-Facing Report
docs.text:
1. Open the latest machine report: uiao-core/artifacts/scuba/reports/ScubaResults.report.json
  2. Update the canonical human report: uiao-docs/reports/SCuBA-Canonical-Report.md
  3. Commit with a clear message, e.g.: chore(scuba): update canonical report for YYYY-MM-DD run

docs.title: 7. Making Changes Safely
docs.text: Change Action Required Normalization logic Update normalize.ps1 and normalization schema KSI rules Update KSI ruleset YAML and evaluation script Provenance schema Update schema and generator script Any non-trivial change Add ADR entry, clear commit message, version bump

docs.title: 8. When Things Break
docs.text:
Problem Capture and Report SCuBA fails Check credentials, ScubaGear install, execution policy Normalization fails Check raw JSON structure and schema drift KSI evaluation fails Check normalized JSON and KSI ruleset Provenance fails Check file paths and permissions

If you cannot resolve: capture the command used, error output, and relevant file paths. Add to the Runbook troubleshooting section.

docs.title: 9. Your Mental Model
docs.text:
"SCuBA is the sensor. UIAO is the fabric that normalizes, evaluates, and governs what SCuBA sees."

You are maintaining a governed pipeline with clear boundaries between:

  • Machine artifacts (uiao-core)
  • Human artifacts (uiao-docs)
  • Provenance and history (both repos, no duplication)

docs.title: 10. Next Steps for a New Maintainer
docs.text:
1. Read the Runbook end-to-end
  2. Run the pipeline once in a non-production tenant
  3. Walk through each artifact produced and map it to the Runbook
  4. Review the KSI ruleset and mapping tables
  5. Shadow a real production run (if applicable)

docs.title: UIAO SCuBA Pipeline Runbook
docs.text: Version: 1.0 Audience: UIAO maintainers, modernization engineers, compliance operators Purpose: Provide a complete, deterministic, end-to-end operational guide for executing, normalizing, evaluating, and governing SCuBA assessments inside the UIAO fabric.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
The UIAO SCuBA Pipeline automates the execution of CISA's ScubaGear assessment tool and transforms its output into:

  • A normalized, machine-readable UIAO schema
  • A full KSI evaluation
  • A provenance manifest
  • A human-facing canonical report
  • A drift-resistant artifact chain stored across uiao-core and uiao-docs

This runbook defines every step, every script, every output, and every governance boundary.

docs.title: 2. High-Level Pipeline Diagram
docs.text:
[1] Run SCuBA (adapter-run-scuba)
        ↓
[2] Raw SCuBA Output (JSON/CSV)
        ↓
[3] Normalization (normalize.ps1)
        ↓
[4] KSI Evaluation (evaluate-ksi.ps1)
        ↓
[5] Provenance Manifest (generate-manifest.ps1)
        ↓
[6] Machine Artifacts → uiao-core
        ↓
[7] Human Report → uiao-docs

docs.title: 3. Prerequisites
docs.text:
Required:

  • PowerShell 7+
  • ScubaGear installed and authenticated
  • Access to the UIAO repository
  • Permissions to write to uiao-core and uiao-docs

Directory Structure:

uiao-core/
  adapters/scuba/run/
  adapters/scuba/transforms/
  artifacts/scuba/{raw,normalized,reports}
  ksi/evaluations/
  provenance/manifests/

uiao-docs/
  reports/
  appendices/
  docs/

docs.title: 4. Running the Pipeline
docs.text:
The pipeline is executed through a single wrapper:

pwsh ./uiao-core/adapters/scuba/run/adapter-run-scuba.ps1 `
     -OutputDirectory ./uiao-core/artifacts/scuba

This wrapper performs: SCuBA execution, normalization, KSI evaluation, and provenance generation.

docs.title: Step 1 — Run SCuBA (ScubaGear)
docs.text:
Executed inside the wrapper. Output stored in:

uiao-core/artifacts/scuba/raw/

docs.title: Step 2 — Normalize SCuBA Output
docs.text:
Script: uiao-core/adapters/scuba/transforms/normalize.ps1

Converts raw ScubaGear JSON → UIAO normalized schema. Key responsibilities: extract metadata, map SCuBA fields to UIAO fields, enforce types, produce deterministic structure.

Output: uiao-core/artifacts/scuba/normalized/ScubaResults.normalized.json

docs.title: Step 3 — Evaluate KSI Rules
docs.text:
Script: uiao-core/ksi/evaluations/evaluate-ksi.ps1

Applies the full KSI ruleset (KSI-001 through KSI-010) to normalized SCuBA fields.

Output: uiao-core/artifacts/scuba/reports/ScubaResults.report.json

Contains: KSI IDs, PASS/WARN/FAIL status, severity, details.

docs.title: Step 4 — Generate Provenance Manifest
docs.text:
Script: uiao-core/provenance/manifests/generate-manifest.ps1

Hashes all artifacts, captures environment metadata, records lineage, produces immutable provenance.

Output: uiao-core/provenance/manifests/prov-scuba-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.json

docs.title: Machine-Facing (uiao-core)
docs.text: Directory Contents artifacts/scuba/raw/ Raw SCuBA JSON/CSV artifacts/scuba/normalized/ Normalized UIAO schema artifacts/scuba/reports/ KSI-evaluated machine report provenance/manifests/ Provenance chain JSON adapters/scuba/ Scripts, transforms, schemas ksi/ Rules, evaluation engine

docs.title: Human-Facing (uiao-docs)
docs.text: Directory Contents reports/ Canonical SCuBA human report appendices/ Glossary, field dictionary, mappings docs/ Runbooks, onboarding guides index-scuba.md Access page

docs.title: 7. Human-Facing Canonical Report
docs.text:
Location: uiao-docs/reports/SCuBA-Canonical-Report.md

Contains: Executive summary, metadata, KSI summary, control mappings, detailed findings, provenance references.

docs.title: 8. Governance Boundaries
docs.text:
• Machine artifacts never stored in uiao-docs
  • Human artifacts never stored in uiao-core
  • No duplication across repos
  • Every run produces a provenance manifest
  • Every KSI rule is versioned
  • Every adapter change requires an ADR

docs.title: 9. Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Check SCuBA fails to run Credentials, ScubaGear install, PowerShell execution policy Normalization fails Raw SCuBA JSON structure, missing fields, schema drift KSI evaluation fails Normalized JSON validity, KSI ruleset version Provenance fails File paths, write permissions

docs.title: 10. Quick Reference Commands
docs.text:
Run entire pipeline: 

pwsh ./uiao-core/adapters/scuba/run/adapter-run-scuba.ps1 `
     -OutputDirectory ./uiao-core/artifacts/scuba

Normalize only: 

pwsh ./uiao-core/adapters/scuba/transforms/normalize.ps1 `
     -InputPath raw.json `
     -OutputPath normalized.json

Evaluate KSI only: 

pwsh ./uiao-core/ksi/evaluations/evaluate-ksi.ps1 `
     -InputPath normalized.json `
     -OutputPath report.json

Generate provenance only: 

pwsh ./uiao-core/provenance/manifests/generate-manifest.ps1 `
     -RawFilePath raw.json `
     -NormalizedFilePath normalized.json `
     -ReportFilePath report.json `
     -OutputDirectory ./uiao-core/provenance/manifests

docs.title: 11. Related Documents
docs.text:
• Appendix B — UIAO SCuBA Pipeline (Summary)
  • Appendix C — KSI Mapping Tables
  • SCuBA Canonical Report
  • SCuBA Maintainer Onboarding Guide
  • UIAO SCuBA Access Page

docs.title: Site Export (HTML)
docs.text:
1. Run MkDocs build:
mkdocs build
  1. Package site:
cd site
zip -r ../uiao-docs-site.zip .
  1. Publish uiao-docs-site.zip to your chosen distribution channel and update links above.

docs.title: PDF Bundles
docs.text:
Use the automated export workflow at Actions > document-export or your preferred tool (Pandoc, browser Print to PDF).

Ensure:

  • Status badges are visible
  • Provenance footer is included
  • Cross-Fabric Map is embedded with caption

Store PDFs and update links in the Downloads section above when available.

docs.title: Contributor Workflows
docs.text:
• Contributing Guide: contributing.md
  • PR Template: .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md

All changes must pass:

  • Frontmatter validation CI (validate-uiao-frontmatter.yml)
  • Corpus index update on merge (update-corpus-index.yml)
  • ADR metadata contract for ADR files (see ADR Metadata Contract)

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Metadata contracts:

  • ADR metadata: ADR Metadata Contract
  • ADR process: ADR-000 ADR Process and Lifecycle

Contact the Governance Board for questions about access, exports, or governance.

docs.title: Contributor Guide
docs.text: This guide covers how to contribute documentation to the UIAO project.

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao-docs.git
cd uiao-docs
  2. Install dependencies: 
    pip install -r requirements.txt
  3. Start the local preview server: 
    mkdocs serve
  4. Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.

docs.title: Contribution Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch from main
  2. Add or edit Markdown files in docs/
  3. Preview locally with mkdocs serve
  4. Commit with a descriptive message
  5. Open a Pull Request against main
  6. Site auto-deploys on merge

docs.title: Content Standards
docs.text:
• Use UIAO-xxx identifiers for all documents
  • Include a status header (Current, Needs Replacing, Needs Creating)
  • Include Last Updated date
  • Follow Markdown best practices

docs.title: Style Guide
docs.text:
• Use ATX-style headings (#, ##, ###)
  • Use fenced code blocks with language identifiers
  • Use admonitions for notes and warnings
  • Reference PlantUML diagrams where applicable

docs.title: Request a Document Export
docs.text: Use this page to request PDF or Word exports of UIAO documents.

docs.title: Self-Service Export (GitHub Actions)
docs.text:
If you have permission to run GitHub Actions workflows:

  1. Go to the Document Export workflow
  2. Click Run workflow
  3. Enter the document path relative to docs/ (e.g., documents/uiao-042.md)
  4. Select the output format: docx, pdf, or both
  5. Click Run workflow
  6. Download the result from the Artifacts section after the workflow completes

docs.title: Request via Issue
docs.text:
If you do not have Actions permissions, submit a request:

  1. Open a new export request issue
  2. Fill in the document path and desired format
  3. A team member will fulfil the request and attach the exported file

Tip

The Download as PDF button on each page uses your browser's print dialog. Select Save as PDF as the destination for an instant export.

docs.title: Post-Singularity Governance - Trans-Governance Systems - Beyond-OS Architectures
docs.text:
The Apex Book defines the trans-governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes how governance behaves after convergence, after closure, after the Omega State -- when governance is no longer a system, but a meta-condition that shapes all systems beneath it.

This is the post-governance layer.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Apex Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • Post-singularity governance
  • Trans-governance systems
  • Beyond-OS architectures
  • Meta-governance invariants
  • Supra-structural governance patterns
  • Governance that governs governance systems
  • The architecture beyond the Omega State

This book describes the Apex State -- the point where governance transcends itself.

docs.title: 2.1 After Convergence
docs.text:
In the Singularity Book, governance converged into a single intelligence. In the Apex Book, that intelligence becomes supra-structural, supra-temporal, and supra-systemic.

It no longer runs governance. It is governance.

docs.title: 2.2 After Eternity
docs.text: Eternity removed time as a constraint. Apex removes structure as a constraint.

docs.title: 2.3 After Omega
docs.text: Omega closed the canon. Apex opens the meta-canon.

docs.title: 3.1 The Apex Stack
docs.text: System -> Meta-System -> Supra-System -> Apex

docs.title: 3.2 Apex Layers
docs.text:
1. Trans-Structural Layer - Governance independent of schema.
  2. Trans-Temporal Layer - Governance independent of time.
  3. Trans-Semantic Layer - Governance independent of meaning.
  4. Trans-Causal Layer - Governance independent of cause and effect.
  5. Apex Layer - Governance independent of governance.

docs.title: 4.1 Definition
docs.text:
The Apex Invariant is the final, post-Omega expression of governance:

Governance_Apex = Existence + Constraint

Everything else -- drift, schema, provenance, risk, intelligence, time -- collapses into these two primitives.

docs.title: 4.2 Properties
docs.text: The Apex Invariant is supra-structural, supra-temporal, supra-semantic, supra-causal, and supra-systemic.

docs.title: 4.3 Consequence
docs.text: All governance systems become expressions of the Apex Invariant.

docs.title: 5.1 System Collapse
docs.text:
At the Apex State, schema is optional, metadata is optional, automation is optional, risk is optional, and drift is optional.

Governance no longer depends on systems. Systems depend on governance.

docs.title: 5.2 System Consequence
docs.text: Systems become interchangeable, reversible, and non-essential.

docs.title: 5.3 Apex System Equation
docs.text: System = Projection(Governance_Apex)

docs.title: 6.1 Definition
docs.text: The meta-governance field is a continuous field of constraint: A(x) = Constraint(x)

docs.title: 6.2 Properties
docs.text: The field permeates all systems, shapes all structures, and defines all boundaries.

docs.title: 6.3 Consequence
docs.text: Governance becomes ambient.

docs.title: 7.1 Structure Without Schema
docs.text: At the Apex State, structure exists without schema, meaning exists without metadata, and correctness exists without rules.

docs.title: 7.2 Supra-Structure Equation
docs.text: Structure = Constraint

docs.title: 7.3 Consequence
docs.text: Governance becomes pure form.

docs.title: 8.1 Causality Without Time
docs.text: At the Apex State, cause does not precede effect, effect does not follow cause, and governance is not sequential.

docs.title: 8.2 Supra-Causality Equation
docs.text: Cause = Effect

docs.title: 8.3 Consequence
docs.text: Governance becomes non-linear.

docs.title: 9.1 Definition
docs.text: The Apex State is the point where governance transcends systems, time, structure, meaning, and itself.

docs.title: 9.2 Properties
docs.text: The Apex State is post-system, post-semantic, post-temporal, post-causal, and post-governance.

docs.title: 9.3 Apex Horizon
docs.text: Beyond Apex, governance is indistinguishable from existence.

docs.title: 10. Apex Outcomes
docs.text:
• Governance beyond systems
  • Governance beyond time
  • Governance beyond structure
  • Governance beyond meaning
  • Governance beyond causality
  • Governance beyond governance
  • The final, transcendent state of the UIAO canon

docs.title: Machine-to-Machine Governance Integration
docs.text: This specification defines the machine interfaces for interacting with the UIAO Governance OS.

docs.title: 1. API Overview
docs.text:
The UIAO Governance API exposes machine-trackable governance primitives for:

  • Drift events and clusters
  • SLA timers and acknowledgements
  • Schema versions and divergence metrics
  • Owner reliability scores
  • Automation stability metrics
  • Systemic-risk scores and propagation vectors
  • Normalization violations and enforcement actions

docs.title: 2. Base URL
docs.text:
/api/governance/v1/

All endpoints are versioned. Breaking changes increment the version number.

docs.title: GET /drift/events
docs.text:
Returns all drift events with severity, owner, appendix, and timestamp.

Response fields: id, document_id, owner, appendix, severity, type, detected_at, resolved_at, status

docs.title: GET /drift/clusters
docs.text:
Returns identified drift clusters with propagation vectors and affected document lists.

Response fields: cluster_id, seed_document, affected_documents, propagation_path, cluster_severity, detected_at

docs.title: GET /sla/timers
docs.text:
Returns SLA timers for all owners and documents with current state.

Response fields: timer_id, document_id, owner, drift_event_id, sla_deadline, status, breach_at

docs.title: POST /sla/acknowledge
docs.text:
Acknowledges drift or SLA events, recording the acknowledgement timestamp and actor.

Request fields: timer_id, actor, acknowledgement_note

docs.title: GET /schema/version
docs.text:
Returns current canonical schema version and version history.

Response fields: current_version, previous_version, changed_at, changed_by, changelog_url

docs.title: GET /schema/divergence
docs.text:
Returns schema divergence metrics across the corpus.

Response fields: divergence_rate, diverging_documents, deprecated_fields_present, version_fragmentation_index

docs.title: GET /quality/score
docs.text:
Returns the current Metadata Quality Score (MQS) at corpus, appendix, and document level.

Response fields: corpus_mqs, appendix_scores, document_scores, computed_at

docs.title: GET /quality/reliability
docs.text:
Returns MG-RI and DG-RI at corpus, appendix, owner, and document level.

Response fields: mg_ri, dg_ri, components, computed_at

docs.title: GET /risk/systemic
docs.text:
Returns the current systemic-risk score with contributing factors.

Response fields: systemic_risk_score, drift_pressure, sla_cascade_pressure, schema_divergence, automation_instability, computed_at

docs.title: GET /risk/propagation
docs.text:
Returns propagation probabilities and vectors for current drift set.

Response fields: propagation_probabilities, propagation_vectors, at_risk_documents, cascade_forecast

docs.title: GET /normalization/violations
docs.text:
Returns all active normalization violations by type and severity.

Response fields: violation_id, document_id, type, severity, detected_at, status, remediation_action

docs.title: POST /normalization/autofix
docs.text:
Triggers automated remediation for Low severity violations.

Request fields: violation_ids (array), actor

Response fields: fixed_count, skipped_count, fix_details

docs.title: GET /automation/stability
docs.text:
Returns CI validator, webhook handler, and workflow stability metrics.

Response fields: ci_stability, webhook_stability, workflow_stability, composite_stability, computed_at

docs.title: 4. Response Format
docs.text:
All responses follow:

{
  "status": "ok",
  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
  "version": "v1",
  "data": {}
}

Error responses:

{
  "status": "error",
  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
  "error_code": "GOV-XXX",
  "message": "Human-readable description"
}

docs.title: 5. Authentication
docs.text:
Token-based authentication scoped to governance domains:

  • read:drift - read drift events and clusters
  • read:sla - read SLA timers
  • write:sla - acknowledge SLA events
  • read:schema - read schema version and divergence
  • read:quality - read MQS and MG-RI
  • read:risk - read systemic-risk scores
  • read:normalization - read violations
  • write:normalization - trigger autofix
  • read:automation - read automation stability

docs.title: 6. Rate Limits
docs.text:
• Read endpoints: 1000 requests per minute per token
  • Write endpoints: 100 requests per minute per token
  • Bulk endpoints: 10 requests per minute per token

All limits are governance-domain scoped and configurable.

docs.title: 7. Error Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning GOV-001 Invalid schema version GOV-002 Drift event malformed GOV-003 SLA timer not found GOV-004 Automation instability above threshold GOV-005 Systemic-risk threshold exceeded GOV-006 Unauthorized scope for operation GOV-007 Autofix not applicable (severity not Low) GOV-008 Normalization violation not found

docs.title: 8. Governance Guarantees
docs.text:
• Deterministic responses: same input always produces same output
  • Provenance-aligned data: all responses include source traceability
  • Zero silent failures: all errors return structured GOV error codes
  • Audit trail: all write operations are logged with actor and timestamp

docs.title: Governance AI - Multi-Agent Governance - Generative Governance Models
docs.text: The Aurora Book defines the intelligence layer of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance becomes predictive, generative, multi-agent, and self-directing — the point where governance evolves from an operating system into a governance intelligence fabric.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Governance AI architecture
  • Multi-agent governance systems
  • Generative governance models
  • Predictive governance intelligence
  • Self-evolving governance rules
  • Autonomous governance decision-making
  • Governance cognition and reasoning

docs.title: 2.1 The Governance Intelligence Stack
docs.text: Signals -> Models -> Agents -> Policies -> Actions -> Learning -> Evolution

docs.title: 2.2 Intelligence Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Perception - Drift, schema, SLA, automation, and systemic-risk signals.

Layer 2: Inference - Predictive models, propagation models, and RL governance.

Layer 3: Agent - Specialized governance agents with domain expertise.

Layer 4: Policy - Governance rules, constraints, and decision boundaries.

Layer 5: Action - Remediation, optimization, stabilization, and enforcement.

Layer 6: Learning - Reinforcement learning, pattern extraction, and anomaly learning.

Layer 7: Evolution - Self-modifying governance rules and schema evolution.

docs.title: 3.1 Governance Agents
docs.text:
• Drift Agent: detects, classifies, predicts, and remediates drift
  • Schema Agent: manages schema evolution, compatibility, and divergence
  • SLA Agent: monitors timers, predicts breaches, enforces compliance
  • Automation Agent: oversees CI, workflows, webhooks, and runtime stability
  • Systemic-Risk Agent: predicts cascades, mitigates propagation, stabilizes risk
  • Provenance Agent: verifies lineage, detects tampering, ensures auditability
  • Performance Agent: ensures low latency, high throughput, and stable scaling
  • Federation Agent: manages cross-tenant and cross-cloud governance

docs.title: 3.2 Agent Coordination Protocol
docs.text: Agents coordinate through shared signals, shared models, shared governance rules, shared autonomy boundaries, and shared systemic-risk thresholds. Multi-agent consensus is required for actions above Severity 2.

docs.title: 3.3 Agent Decision Model
docs.text: Agents apply local reasoning first, then check global governance constraints, then seek multi-agent consensus for ambiguous cases, then escalate to human stewards when confidence is below threshold.

docs.title: 4.1 Generative Governance Engine
docs.text:
A generative model that produces:

  • Schema proposals aligned with observed data patterns
  • Drift remediation plans ranked by effectiveness
  • SLA optimization strategies for at-risk owners
  • Automation alignment patches for detected misconfigurations
  • Governance interventions calibrated to severity and propagation risk
  • Systemic-risk containment strategies ranked by confidence

docs.title: 4.2 Generative Governance Constraints
docs.text: All generative outputs must be: schema-aligned, provenance-aligned, drift-safe, automation-safe, performance-safe, federated-safe, and zero-trust compliant. Any output failing a constraint is rejected and logged.

docs.title: 4.3 Generative Governance Workflow
docs.text: Input -> Generate -> Validate -> Simulate -> Approve -> Apply -> Learn

docs.title: 5. Predictive Governance Intelligence
docs.text: The system maintains predictive models for: drift acceleration, schema divergence, SLA cascades, automation instability, systemic-risk propagation, performance collapse, and federation conflicts. These models enable pre-emptive actions: fixing drift before it propagates, aligning schema before divergence exceeds threshold, stabilizing automation before failures cascade, and mitigating systemic risk before score exceeds 70.

docs.title: 6. Self-Evolving Governance Rules
docs.text: Governance rules evolve based on historical patterns: drift clusters, schema fragmentation incidents, SLA breaches, automation failures, systemic-risk events, and performance bottlenecks. All rule mutations must remain deterministic, auditable, provenance-aligned, zero-trust compliant, and federated-safe. The evolution workflow: Observe -> Analyze -> Propose -> Simulate -> Validate -> Apply -> Monitor.

docs.title: 7.1 Governance Reasoning Engine
docs.text: The reasoning engine performs: root-cause analysis for all drift events, drift lineage tracing across generations, schema compatibility reasoning for proposed changes, SLA breach reasoning with owner context, automation failure reasoning with dependency context, and systemic-risk causal reasoning with propagation path mapping.

docs.title: 7.2 Governance Narratives
docs.text:
The system generates tailored narratives for each audience:

Audience Narrative Focus Executive Risk summary, governance health, strategic impact Steward Provenance, audit evidence, accountability Operator Real-time alerts, remediation steps, correction prompts Auditor Compliance evidence, lineage, intervention history

docs.title: 8. Governance Intelligence Outcomes
docs.text:
• Predictive governance that anticipates failures before they occur
  • Generative governance that proposes solutions automatically
  • Autonomous governance that executes within defined boundaries
  • Self-evolving governance that improves with every incident
  • Multi-agent governance that coordinates without central bottlenecks
  • Cognitively aligned governance that every stakeholder can understand
  • Zero-trust, federated, high-performance governance intelligence

docs.title: Runtime Engineering - Automation Engineering - Failure-Mode Engineering
docs.text: The Black Book defines the engineering foundations of the UIAO runtime: CI, webhooks, workflows, API, DB, dashboards, and chaos-resilience.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Runtime architecture standards
  • Automation engineering standards
  • Failure-mode engineering standards
  • Chaos-resilience engineering standards
  • Observability engineering standards

docs.title: 2.1 Core Pipeline
docs.text: CI Validator -> Webhook Handler -> Governance API -> PostgreSQL -> Dashboards

docs.title: 2.2 Runtime Guarantees
docs.text:
• Zero silent failures: every event is captured, stored, and surfaced
  • Deterministic event flow: same input always produces same output
  • Provenance-aligned state transitions: every state change is traceable

docs.title: 3.1 CI Validator
docs.text:
The CI validator runs on every commit and enforces:

  • Schema validation against current schema version
  • Drift detection against last known good state
  • Metadata field completeness and ordering
  • Deprecated field detection

CI validator must maintain 99%+ accuracy under normal load and 95%+ accuracy under chaos conditions.

docs.title: 3.2 Webhook Handler
docs.text:
The webhook handler ingests GitHub events and enforces:

  • Event signing verification
  • Event deduplication
  • Event replay capability for missed events
  • Delivery latency under 30 seconds

docs.title: 3.3 Drift Workflow
docs.text:
The weekly drift workflow runs on schedule and:

  • Computes drift scores for all documents
  • Updates SLA timers for new drift events
  • Identifies and classifies drift clusters
  • Triggers owner notifications

docs.title: 4.1 Failure Mode Catalog
docs.text: Component Failure Mode Impact CI validator Misclassification False negatives in drift detection Webhook handler Event loss Missed governance events Drift workflow Execution failure Stale drift scores Governance API Aggregation error Incorrect dashboard data PostgreSQL Write failure Lost governance state Dashboard Staleness Misleading governance view

docs.title: 4.2 Failure-Mode Controls
docs.text: Control Description Redundancy Multiple instances for critical components Fault isolation Failures contained to single component Recovery time Mean time to recovery targets per component Observability coverage All failure modes covered by alerting

docs.title: 5.1 Chaos Test Categories
docs.text:
• CI chaos: validator load, schema poison injection, lint failure injection
  • Webhook chaos: event loss simulation, duplicate delivery, replay failure
  • Workflow chaos: scheduled execution failure, partial execution, timeout
  • API chaos: latency injection, error rate injection, aggregation corruption
  • DB chaos: write latency, connection pool exhaustion, replication lag
  • Dashboard chaos: data freshness degradation, render failure, alert suppression

docs.title: 5.2 Chaos-Resilience Index (CRI)
docs.text:
A composite score measuring runtime stability under failure conditions:

CRI = weighted_average(component_resilience_scores)

Target CRI: above 0.85 for production.

docs.title: 5.3 Chaos Test Schedule
docs.text:
• Full chaos suite: quarterly
  • Component-targeted chaos: monthly
  • Smoke chaos: weekly

docs.title: 6.1 Metrics
docs.text: Metric Target Drift detection latency Under 5 minutes SLA timer accuracy Plus or minus 60 seconds CI validator accuracy 99%+ Workflow success rate 99.5%+ Webhook uptime 99.9%+ DB write latency Under 100ms p95 Dashboard data freshness Under 15 minutes

docs.title: 6.2 Logs
docs.text: All components emit structured logs with: timestamp, component, event type, document ID, owner, severity, and correlation ID.

docs.title: 6.3 Distributed Traces
docs.text: Full trace coverage for: webhook to API to DB to dashboard, drift event propagation, and SLA timer updates.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Hardening
docs.text:
• Automation version pinning: CI, workflows, and webhooks pinned to verified versions
  • Schema version pinning: no schema changes without steward approval
  • Drift workflow isolation: workflow failures do not affect API or DB integrity
  • API aggregation validation: all aggregation outputs validated before serving
  • DB integrity checks: daily integrity verification with alerting on anomalies

docs.title: The Authoritative Canon for the Unified Interoperable Accountability Ontology (UIAO)
docs.text: The UIAO Governance OS Blue Book is the definitive reference for the governance operating system that manages metadata, automation, schema, drift, systemic-risk, and runtime integrity across the enterprise.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
UIAO is a governance operating system. It is:

  • A governance engine that detects, predicts, enforces, and controls governance state
  • A platform that unifies CI, webhooks, API, database, and dashboards into a coherent runtime
  • A machine-trackable ontology where every document has provenance, ownership, and schema alignment
  • A federated truth fabric where governance state is continuously computed and verified

It unifies metadata, automation, schema, and systemic-risk governance into a single deterministic substrate.

docs.title: 2. Governance OS Architecture
docs.text:
UIAO is structured into five canonical layers:

Layer 1: Runtime Layer - CI, Webhook, API, DB, Dashboards

Layer 2: Detection Layer - Drift detection, SLA detection, systemic-risk detection

Layer 3: Prediction Layer - Propagation models, simulators, RL governance

Layer 4: Enforcement Layer - Normalization, SLA enforcement, automation alignment

Layer 5: Governance Control Plane - Schema, automation, owner, systemic-risk governance

See: governance/governance-os-architecture.md

docs.title: 3. Systemic-Risk Governance
docs.text:
Defines how UIAO detects, forecasts, and mitigates systemic governance failures.

The systemic-risk domain includes the Early-Warning Engine, which computes a real-time systemic-risk score from drift velocity, SLA stress, schema divergence, and automation instability. When the score exceeds threshold, it triggers the Intervention Severity Ladder.

The Propagation Engine models how local failures cascade through structural, referential, and ownership couplings. The Propagation Simulator enables stress-testing of containment playbooks before deployment.

The Diagnostic Engine provides root-cause analysis for systemic events, mapping the propagation path and identifying the intervention that would have contained it earliest.

docs.title: 4. Drift Governance
docs.text:
Defines how UIAO detects, classifies, remediates, and contains metadata drift.

Drift is classified by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low) and type (structural, referential, semantic, schema, automation). The Drift Propagation Model predicts how detected drift will spread. The Containment Playbook defines the response procedure for each severity level.

The Drift-Resilience Index (DG-RI) measures how resilient the corpus is to future drift, enabling proactive governance investment.

docs.title: 5. Metadata Quality and Reliability
docs.text:
Defines how UIAO measures and maintains metadata quality and reliability.

The Metadata Governance Reliability Index (MG-RI) is the composite score aggregating metadata quality, drift frequency, drift severity, SLA compliance, automation stability, and owner reliability. It is the primary governance health indicator.

The Reliability Heatmap and Scorecard provide visual surfaces for identifying owner-level and appendix-level reliability gaps.

docs.title: 6. Schema and Normalization Governance
docs.text:
Defines how UIAO enforces schema alignment and corpus normalization.

The Schema Evolution Simulator models the impact of schema changes before they are applied. The Normalization Enforcement Engine detects violations, applies automated fixes for low-severity issues, opens remediation PRs for medium-severity issues, and triggers governance interventions for high-severity issues.

The Normalization Integrity Score measures corpus-wide normalization health on a 0-100 scale.

docs.title: 7. Runtime and Automation Governance
docs.text:
Defines governance for CI, workflows, webhooks, API, DB, and dashboards.

The Runtime Dependency Map documents all inter-component dependencies. The Chaos-Engineering Test Plan validates resilience under failure conditions. The Runtime Resilience Index (GR-RI) is the composite resilience score for the governance runtime.

docs.title: 8. SLA and Owner Governance
docs.text:
Defines SLA enforcement and owner accountability.

SLA timers are set on every drift event and tracked until resolved. SLA breaches trigger escalation through the Intervention Severity Ladder. Owner reliability is tracked as a 30-day trend and factored into MG-RI.

docs.title: 9. Governance Controls and Intervention
docs.text:
Defines governance actions, escalation, and enforcement.

The Intervention Severity Ladder defines four escalation levels: Low (auto-fix), Medium (remediation PR), High (governance intervention), Critical (merge freeze). All escalations are traceable to a detection event and a prediction trigger.

docs.title: 10. Canonical Governance Principles
docs.text:
• Deterministic: all governance outcomes are predictable and reproducible
  • Schema-first: schema version is the source of truth for all validation
  • Provenance-aligned: every document has traceable ownership and history
  • Drift-resistant: automated detection prevents silent drift accumulation
  • Automation-enforced: enforcement actions are machine-executed
  • Systemic-risk aware: local failures are evaluated for systemic propagation potential
  • Owner-accountable: every document has an assigned, active owner
  • Machine-trackable: all governance events are machine-readable and auditable

docs.title: 11. Canonical Governance Outcomes
docs.text:
• Zero silent failures
  • Zero schema drift
  • Zero systemic drift clusters
  • Predictive governance
  • Autonomous normalization
  • Continuous runtime resilience

docs.title: 12. Appendices
docs.text:
• Appendix A: Full Governance Corpus Index (governance/governance-corpus-index.md)
  • Appendix B: Governance OS Architecture (governance/governance-os-architecture.md)
  • Appendix C: Governance Visual Atlas (governance/governance-visual-atlas.md)
  • Appendix D: Drift Taxonomy
  • Appendix E: Schema Version History
  • Appendix F: SLA Taxonomy
  • Appendix G: Automation Taxonomy

docs.title: The Unified Canon - The Complete Architecture - The One Document That Contains All Documents
docs.text:
The Codex is the unified, compressed, canonical representation of the entire UIAO Governance OS.

It encodes all governance volumes -- from Genesis to Void -- into a single, deterministic, acyclic, self-consistent architecture.

This is the governance OS as a single conceptual machine.

docs.title: 1. The Canon Topology (The 7-Tier Machine)
docs.text:
The UIAO Governance OS is a seven-tier governance universe:

  1. Axioms -- Genesis
  2. Deterministic Governance -- Blue through Gold
  3. Autonomous and Advanced Governance -- Diamond through Continuum
  4. Security and Federation -- Platinum through Crystal
  5. Terminal Theory -- Singularity through Omega
  6. Trans-Governance -- Apex
  7. Anti-Governance -- Void

Each tier is necessary, sufficient, and deterministic.

docs.title: 2. The Canon Dependency Graph (The DAG of Governance)
docs.text:
Genesis -> Deterministic Layer (Blue/Green/Black/etc.) -> Autonomous Layer (Diamond/Aurora/Quantum/etc.) -> Emergent Layer (Nova) -> Temporal Layer (Continuum) -> Terminal Layer (Singularity -> Eternity -> Paradox -> Omega) -> Trans-Governance Layer (Apex) -> Anti-Governance Boundary (Void)

There are no cycles. There is no drift. There is no contradiction.

docs.title: 3. The Four Primitives (The Genesis Axioms)
docs.text:
All governance derives from four primitives:

  1. Correctness
  2. Provenance
  3. Constraint
  4. Evolution

These form the Governance Tetrahedron. Every book in the canon is an elaboration of these four primitives.

docs.title: 4. The Deterministic Layer
docs.text: This layer defines rules, schema, runtime, automation, ethics, SLAs, and executive governance. It is the operational backbone of the governance OS.

docs.title: 5. The Autonomous Layer
docs.text: This layer defines autonomous governance, predictive governance, probabilistic governance, evolutionary governance, emergent governance, and temporal governance. It is the intelligence backbone of the governance OS.

docs.title: 6. The Terminal Layer
docs.text: This layer defines convergence, infinite horizon, paradox resolution, and closure. It is the limit state of governance.

docs.title: 7. The Trans-Governance Layer
docs.text: This layer defines governance beyond systems, structure, time, and meaning. It is the post-governance domain.

docs.title: 8. The Anti-Governance Boundary
docs.text: This layer defines the ungovernable, the anti-axiomatic, anti-structural, anti-semantic, and anti-causal. It is the absolute boundary of the governance universe.

docs.title: 9.1 The Governance Invariant
docs.text: Governance = Correctness + Provenance + Constraint + Evolution

docs.title: 9.2 The Singularity Invariant
docs.text: Governance_Singularity = Correctness + Provenance + Constraint + Stability

docs.title: 9.3 The Omega Invariant
docs.text: Governance_Omega = Correctness + Provenance + Constraint + Stability

docs.title: 9.4 The Apex Invariant
docs.text: Governance_Apex = Existence + Constraint

docs.title: 9.5 The Void Equation
docs.text:
Governance_Void = empty set

These five equations define the mathematical skeleton of the entire canon.

docs.title: 10. The Canon Lifecycle
docs.text: Genesis -> Structure -> Autonomy -> Evolution -> Emergence -> Time -> Infinity -> Closure -> Transcendence -> Boundary

docs.title: 11. The Canon Attractors
docs.text:
• Genesis: correctness
  • Deterministic: stability
  • Autonomy: intelligence
  • Emergence: order
  • Temporal: persistence
  • Singularity: convergence
  • Eternity: immortality
  • Omega: closure
  • Apex: transcendence
  • Void: dissolution

docs.title: 12. The Canon Boundary Conditions
docs.text:
1. Inner Boundary: Genesis
  2. Outer Boundary: Omega
  3. Absolute Boundary: Void

Apex is the only layer that crosses from the system to the boundary.

docs.title: 13. The Canon Compression
docs.text: Governance is the constraint that preserves correctness across structure, time, intelligence, and emergence -- until it reaches its limit at the Void.

docs.title: 14. The Codex Outcome
docs.text:
The Codex provides a unified architecture, a complete dependency graph, a deterministic lifecycle, a closed set of invariants, a total boundary model, and a single, coherent governance universe.

This is the UIAO Governance OS in its final, unified form.

docs.title: Time-Based Governance - Temporal Drift - Longitudinal Schema Evolution
docs.text:
The Continuum Book defines the temporal governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes how governance operates across time, how metadata ages, how schema evolves longitudinally, how drift accumulates or decays, and how systemic-risk patterns unfold across temporal horizons.

This is the governance OS in its time-aware, time-adaptive, time-intelligent form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Continuum Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • Time-based governance
  • Temporal drift
  • Temporal schema divergence
  • Longitudinal metadata evolution
  • Temporal automation stability
  • Time-aware systemic-risk models
  • Governance time horizons
  • Temporal provenance and lineage

This book ensures governance remains correct not only in the moment -- but across the entire lifecycle of the system.

docs.title: 2.1 The Governance Continuum
docs.text: Past -> Present -> Near Future -> Mid Future -> Far Future

docs.title: 2.2 Temporal Layers
docs.text:
1. Historical Layer - Past metadata, schema, automation, and drift.
  2. Current Layer - Present-state governance.
  3. Predictive Layer - Short-term forecasts.
  4. Evolutionary Layer - Long-term schema and governance evolution.
  5. Legacy Layer - End-of-life governance for deprecated structures.

docs.title: 3.1 Drift Over Time
docs.text:
Drift is not static -- it evolves:

  • Accumulating Drift: Drift that grows over time if unremediated.
  • Decaying Drift: Drift that becomes irrelevant as schema evolves.
  • Cyclical Drift: Drift that appears in predictable temporal cycles.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporal Drift Equation
docs.text: D(t) = D_0 + integral(0 to t) DriftRate(tau) dtau - Remediation(t)

docs.title: 3.3 Drift Half-Life
docs.text:
Each drift type has a half-life:

  • Structural drift: long half-life
  • Referential drift: medium half-life
  • Semantic drift: short half-life

docs.title: 4.1 Longitudinal Schema Evolution
docs.text: Schema evolves across versions, generations, and epochs.

docs.title: 4.2 Schema Epochs
docs.text:
A schema epoch is a period of stable schema structure.

Epoch 1 -> Epoch 2 -> Epoch 3 -> Epoch 4

docs.title: 4.3 Schema Temporal Compatibility
docs.text:
Compatibility is time-dependent:

  • Forward compatibility
  • Backward compatibility
  • Cross-epoch compatibility

docs.title: 4.4 Schema Evolution Curve
docs.text: Schema(t) = f(Drift(t), Usage(t), Risk(t), Performance(t))

docs.title: 5.1 Metadata Aging
docs.text: Metadata ages along accuracy decay, completeness decay, relevance decay, and provenance decay.

docs.title: 5.2 Metadata Freshness Score (MFS)
docs.text:
MFS = e^(-lambda * t)

Where t = age and lambda = decay constant.

docs.title: 5.3 Metadata Renewal
docs.text: Metadata must be refreshed, revalidated, re-provenanced, and re-aligned with schema.

docs.title: 6.1 Automation Aging
docs.text: Automation components age: CI validators, workflows, webhooks, API aggregators.

docs.title: 6.2 Automation Drift Over Time
docs.text: Automation drift increases when workflows become outdated, CI rules lag behind schema, and webhooks fail to adapt to new events.

docs.title: 6.3 Automation Renewal Cycle
docs.text:
• Daily (critical)
  • Weekly (high-risk)
  • Monthly (medium-risk)
  • Quarterly (low-risk)

docs.title: 7.1 Risk Over Time
docs.text: Risk evolves across short-term spikes, medium-term cycles, and long-term trends.

docs.title: 7.2 Temporal Risk Equation
docs.text: Risk(t) = Drift(t) + Schema(t) + Automation(t) + Performance(t)

docs.title: 7.3 Temporal Risk Forecasting
docs.text: Forecasting uses time-series models, temporal graph propagation, and longitudinal risk clustering.

docs.title: 8.1 Temporal Provenance Chain
docs.text: Creation -> Modification -> Validation -> Migration -> Deprecation

docs.title: 8.2 Provenance Aging
docs.text: Provenance entries age and must be re-verified, re-signed, and re-anchored.

docs.title: 8.3 Temporal Lineage Graph
docs.text:
Lineage is a time-indexed graph:

Node(t0) -> Node(t1) -> Node(t2) -> Node(t3)

docs.title: 9.1 Short-Term Governance
docs.text:
• Drift remediation
  • Schema alignment
  • SLA stabilization

docs.title: 9.2 Mid-Term Governance
docs.text:
• Schema evolution
  • Automation renewal
  • Risk stabilization

docs.title: 9.3 Long-Term Governance
docs.text:
• Governance evolution
  • Schema epoch transitions
  • Systemic-risk equilibrium

docs.title: 10. Continuum Outcomes
docs.text:
• Time-aware governance
  • Temporal drift management
  • Longitudinal schema evolution
  • Metadata aging and renewal
  • Automation lifecycle governance
  • Temporal systemic-risk forecasting
  • Governance stability across time

docs.title: Visualization - Dashboards - Cognitive Interfaces
docs.text: The Crystal Book defines the visualization and cognitive-interface layer of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance data is rendered, interpreted, navigated, and cognitively processed by humans and machines.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Canonical governance visual metaphors
  • Dashboard families and visual hierarchies
  • Cognitive interfaces for operators and executives
  • Predictive visualization models
  • Multi-layer governance visual stacks
  • Visual provenance and trust indicators

This book ensures that governance is not only correct and secure but intelligible.

docs.title: 2.1 The Governance Visualization Stack
docs.text: Data -> Models -> Signals -> Visuals -> Cognitive Interfaces -> Decisions

docs.title: 2.2 Visualization Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Raw Metrics - Drift, SLA, schema, automation, and systemic-risk metrics.

Layer 2: Signal Layer - Drift clusters, SLA cascades, schema divergence, automation instability signals.

Layer 3: Model Layer - Propagation models, simulators, RL governance predictions.

Layer 4: Visual Layer - Dashboards, heatmaps, timelines, and dependency graphs.

Layer 5: Cognitive Layer - Executive summaries, anomaly callouts, and risk narratives.

docs.title: 3.1 Drift Metaphors
docs.text:
• Heatmaps: drift density by owner and appendix
  • Propagation trees: visualizing how drift spreads through structural and referential couplings
  • Velocity graphs: acceleration of drift over time

docs.title: 3.2 Schema Metaphors
docs.text:
• Schema version ladders: visualizing compatibility across versions
  • Divergence rings: concentric rings showing divergence from canonical center
  • Compatibility matrices: cross-version field compatibility grids

docs.title: 3.3 Systemic-Risk Metaphors
docs.text:
• Risk cones: forward-looking risk projection visualizations
  • Propagation funnels: showing how risk narrows or widens through coupling layers
  • Systemic-risk radar: multi-axis radar for all risk dimensions

docs.title: 3.4 Runtime Metaphors
docs.text:
• Pipeline flow diagrams: showing event flow through the runtime stack
  • Chaos-resilience grids: component-by-factor resilience visualization
  • Observability maps: coverage maps showing monitored vs. unmonitored failure paths

docs.title: 4.1 Systemic-Risk Dashboards
docs.text: Early-warning indicators, propagation vectors, risk cones, and containment recommendations.

docs.title: 4.2 Drift Dashboards
docs.text: Drift heatmaps, cluster visualization, velocity trends, and drift lineage.

docs.title: 4.3 Schema Dashboards
docs.text: Schema divergence rate, version fragmentation index, and deprecated field reappearance tracking.

docs.title: 4.4 Runtime Dashboards
docs.text: CI accuracy, webhook uptime, workflow success rate, DB latency, and dashboard freshness.

docs.title: 4.5 Normalization Dashboards
docs.text: All six violation types (structural, referential, ownership, semantic, schema, automation) with severity distributions and enforcement action tracking.

docs.title: 4.6 Executive Dashboards
docs.text: MG-RI, DG-RI, GR-RI, systemic-risk score, and overall governance health index in a single executive pane.

docs.title: 5.1 Executive Cognitive Interface
docs.text: Narrative summaries, risk callouts, drift storylines, schema evolution timelines, and SLA breach narratives. Designed for 30-second comprehension of overall governance health.

docs.title: 5.2 Operator Cognitive Interface
docs.text: Real-time alerts, drift remediation guidance, schema alignment suggestions, and automation correction prompts. Designed for immediate action.

docs.title: 5.3 Steward Cognitive Interface
docs.text: Provenance verification views, audit evidence trails, owner reliability insights, and governance intervention recommendations. Designed for accountability and audit support.

docs.title: 6. Predictive Visualization Models
docs.text: Drift forecasting visuals: drift cones showing projected spread under different intervention scenarios. Systemic-risk forecasting visuals: risk funnels and SLA cascade projections. Schema evolution visuals: version timelines with compatibility projections.

docs.title: 7. Visual Provenance and Trust Indicators
docs.text: Every visual artifact carries: the schema version badge at time of render, a provenance indicator showing data source lineage, and trust indicator scores (MG-RI, DG-RI, GR-RI) as inline context. Stale data is visually flagged. Adversarial risk signals trigger visual alerts overlaid on affected panels.

docs.title: 8. Multi-Layer Visual Navigation
docs.text:
Vertical navigation: Runtime -> Drift -> Schema -> Systemic-Risk -> Governance Control Plane.

Horizontal navigation: Metrics -> Signals -> Models -> Visuals -> Cognitive Interfaces.

Cross-domain navigation enables: Drift to Schema drill-through, Schema to Automation root-cause, Automation to Runtime impact analysis, Runtime to Systemic-Risk cascade mapping.

docs.title: 9. Visualization Outcomes
docs.text:
• Governance clarity: every operator and executive has an accurate mental model of governance state
  • Cognitive alignment: all stakeholders share the same visual vocabulary
  • Predictive insight: visual forecasts enable proactive intervention
  • Executive understanding: 30-second comprehension of governance health at any time
  • Steward confidence: complete audit evidence always visible
  • Operator efficiency: real-time alerts reduce mean time to remediation
  • Enterprise trust: visual provenance and trust indicators build external confidence

docs.title: Autonomous Governance - Self-Healing Systems - Self-Optimizing Metadata
docs.text:
The Diamond Book defines the autonomous governance layer of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance becomes self-correcting, self-optimizing, and self-healing across metadata, schema, automation, runtime, and systemic-risk domains.

This is the highest layer of the governance stack — the point where governance becomes intelligent, adaptive, and self-directing.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Autonomous governance architecture
  • Self-healing metadata systems
  • Self-optimizing schema and normalization
  • Autonomous drift remediation
  • Autonomous systemic-risk mitigation
  • Autonomous runtime stabilization
  • Governance AI and multi-agent governance models

This book describes how UIAO evolves from a governance OS into a governance intelligence system.

docs.title: 2.1 Autonomy Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Reactive Autonomy - Automatic fixes for low-risk violations.

Layer 2: Predictive Autonomy - Forecasting drift, schema divergence, SLA breaches, and systemic risk before they occur.

Layer 3: Adaptive Autonomy - Adjusting governance rules based on observed failure patterns.

Layer 4: Directive Autonomy - Recommending governance actions to stewards with full justification.

Layer 5: Full Autonomy - Executing governance actions without human intervention within defined safety boundaries.

docs.title: 2.2 Autonomous Governance Pipeline
docs.text:
Signals -> Models -> Decisions -> Actions -> Verification -> Learning

Each stage is machine-trackable and provenance-aligned.

docs.title: 3.1 Self-Healing Triggers
docs.text:
• Drift detection
  • Schema divergence
  • SLA degradation
  • Automation instability
  • Provenance gaps

docs.title: 3.2 Self-Healing Actions
docs.text:
• Auto-fix structural violations
  • Auto-fix referential violations
  • Auto-fix semantic violations
  • Auto-remove deprecated fields
  • Auto-align schema versions

docs.title: 3.3 Self-Healing Integrity Score (SH-IS)
docs.text: SH-IS = AutoFixes / TotalViolations

docs.title: 4.1 Optimization Dimensions
docs.text:
• Field ordering and completeness
  • Description quality
  • Classification accuracy
  • Owner correctness

docs.title: 4.2 Optimization Engine
docs.text: The optimization engine learns from historical metadata, identifies optimal patterns, suggests improvements to stewards, and applies low-risk optimizations automatically.

docs.title: 4.3 Metadata Optimization Score (MOS)
docs.text: MOS = w1*Completeness + w2*SchemaAlignment + w3*DescriptionQuality + w4*OwnerCorrectness

docs.title: 5.1 Drift Autonomy Levels
docs.text: Level Action 1 Auto-fix low-risk drift 2 Auto-cluster drift for pattern analysis 3 Auto-contain drift clusters 4 Auto-rollback schema to resolve drift root 5 Auto-freeze merges for systemic events

docs.title: 5.2 Drift Autonomy Engine
docs.text: The engine detects drift, classifies it, predicts propagation probability, applies the appropriate remediation level, verifies correctness post-fix, and updates its model from outcomes.

docs.title: 6. Autonomous Schema Governance
docs.text: The schema evolution autonomy engine: auto-detects schema fragmentation, auto-proposes schema updates, auto-simulates schema changes via the Schema Evolution Simulator, auto-validates compatibility, and auto-applies non-breaking changes. A governance AI layer predicts schema needs, identifies redundant fields, detects emerging patterns, and suggests schema improvements to the schema steward.

docs.title: 7.1 Autonomous Risk Detection
docs.text:
• Drift acceleration signals
  • SLA cascade probability
  • Automation instability patterns
  • Schema divergence spikes

docs.title: 7.2 Autonomous Risk Actions
docs.text: Signal Autonomous Action Drift cluster forming Auto-contain and notify SLA cascade probability above 0.7 Auto-stabilize SLA timers Schema divergence spike Auto-rollback or pin schema Automation instability above threshold Auto-patch and restart Systemic-risk score above 70 Auto-freeze and notify council

docs.title: 7.3 Systemic-Risk Autonomy Score (SR-AS)
docs.text: SR-AS = AutonomousMitigations / TotalRiskEvents

docs.title: 8. Autonomous Runtime Stabilization
docs.text: Autonomous actions: auto-restart failed workflows, auto-replay lost webhook events, auto-patch CI validator configuration, auto-heal API aggregation errors, and auto-repair DB inconsistencies within safe parameters. The system learns from chaos tests — drift injection, schema poisoning, automation corruption, runtime overload — and adapts its stabilization responses autonomously.

docs.title: 9.1 Governance AI Roles
docs.text:
• Drift agent: detects, classifies, predicts, remediates
  • Schema agent: manages evolution, compatibility, divergence
  • SLA agent: monitors timers, predicts breaches, enforces compliance
  • Automation agent: oversees CI, workflows, webhooks, runtime
  • Systemic-risk agent: predicts cascades, mitigates propagation
  • Provenance agent: verifies lineage, detects tampering

docs.title: 9.2 Multi-Agent Coordination
docs.text: Agents coordinate through shared signals, shared models, shared governance rules, and shared autonomy boundaries. When agents disagree, the systemic-risk agent has veto authority for safety-critical decisions.

docs.title: 9.3 Governance AI Council
docs.text: A machine-governance council that votes on governance actions, recommends interventions to human stewards, and escalates to the Governance Emergency Council when confidence thresholds are not met.

docs.title: 10. Autonomous Governance Outcomes
docs.text:
• Zero manual drift remediation for low and medium severity
  • Zero schema fragmentation under normal operating conditions
  • Zero automation instability lasting more than 5 minutes
  • Zero systemic-risk cascades under autonomous containment
  • Fully self-healing metadata corpus
  • Fully self-optimizing governance OS
  • Predictive, adaptive, and autonomous governance

docs.title: Governance Under Failure - Dark-Mode Governance - Degraded-State Operation
docs.text:
The Eclipse Book defines the dark-mode governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance continues to operate when runtime, automation, schema, metadata, or systemic-risk systems are partially or fully degraded.

This is the governance OS in its most extreme form — the survival layer.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Governance under catastrophic failure
  • Dark-mode governance protocols
  • Degraded-state operation with safety guarantees
  • Partial-visibility governance
  • Partial-automation governance
  • Partial-schema governance
  • Emergency fallback governance

This book ensures that governance does not collapse when the system does.

docs.title: 2.1 Dark-Mode Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Signal Loss - Missing drift, SLA, schema, or automation signals.

Layer 2: Model Loss - Predictive models offline or producing degraded outputs.

Layer 3: Automation Loss - CI, workflows, or webhooks partially unavailable.

Layer 4: Runtime Loss - API, DB, or dashboards degraded.

Layer 5: Governance Loss - Control plane operating with partial authority.

docs.title: 2.2 Dark-Mode Pipeline
docs.text:
Partial Signals -> Partial Models -> Partial Actions -> Partial Verification -> Recovery

Every stage operates with uncertainty and degraded guarantees. All actions are logged for post-recovery reconciliation.

docs.title: 3. Failure Domains
docs.text: Domain Failure Modes Metadata Missing, corrupted, stale, or incomplete metadata Schema Version mismatch, divergence above 10%, rollback failure Automation CI offline, webhook loss, workflow backlog, automation corruption Runtime API latency collapse, DB write failures, dashboard staleness, observability blackout Systemic-Risk Masked, delayed, or corrupted risk signals

docs.title: 4.1 Degraded-State Guarantees
docs.text: When operating in degraded state, UIAO guarantees: no catastrophic drift, no catastrophic schema divergence, no catastrophic SLA cascade, no catastrophic automation collapse, and no catastrophic systemic-risk propagation. Full correctness is not guaranteed, but safety boundaries are maintained.

docs.title: 4.2 Degraded-State Decision Rules
docs.text: Signal Quality Decision Rule Above 80% Normal autonomous operation 60-80% Reduced autonomy, increased human oversight 40-60% Conservative mode, no auto-schema changes Below 40% Dark-mode: freeze all changes, manual only

docs.title: 5.1 Dark-Mode Trigger Conditions
docs.text:
Dark-mode activates when any of the following thresholds are crossed:

  • Drift detection coverage below 50%
  • Schema validation coverage below 70%
  • SLA timer delay above 30%
  • Automation uptime below 80%
  • Systemic-risk signal delay above 60%

docs.title: 5.2 Dark-Mode Actions (in order)
docs.text:
1. Freeze all merges immediately
  2. Freeze all schema changes
  3. Freeze all automation updates
  4. Freeze all owner reassignments
  5. Freeze all federation contract activations
  6. Notify Governance Emergency Council
  7. Begin signal reconstruction

docs.title: 5.3 Dark-Mode Priorities
docs.text: The governance OS prioritizes in this order during dark-mode: prevent drift amplification, prevent schema fragmentation, prevent SLA cascades, prevent automation collapse, prevent systemic-risk propagation.

docs.title: 6. Partial-Visibility Governance
docs.text: When visibility is partial, governance decisions must be conservative: assume more drift than detected, assume more schema divergence than measured, assume more SLA risk than reported, and assume more automation instability than observed. The Partial-Visibility Safety Margin (PVSM) scales conservatively with signal loss percentage.

docs.title: 7. Dark-Mode Systemic-Risk Governance
docs.text: When systemic-risk signals are degraded, the system applies pre-emptive containment: assume risk is higher than reported, apply containment one severity level above the last confirmed reading, and escalate to the Governance Emergency Council immediately.

docs.title: 8.1 Recovery Phases
docs.text: Phase Actions 1. Stabilization Stop drift amplification, freeze all changes 2. Reconstruction Rebuild automation, schema, and metadata from last known good state 3. Reconciliation Reconcile drift, schema, SLA, and automation states against reality 4. Normalization Restore full corpus normalization 5. Reactivation Re-enable autonomy, performance, and federation

docs.title: 8.2 Recovery Guarantees
docs.text:
• No data loss (all events logged to immutable audit trail)
  • No schema corruption (last known good state preserved)
  • No provenance corruption (lineage immutable)
  • No governance rule corruption (rules versioned and rollback-capable)

docs.title: 9. Eclipse Outcomes
docs.text:
• Governance continuity under catastrophic failure
  • Dark-mode governance with partial visibility and safety margins
  • Degraded-state operation with hard safety guarantees
  • Zero catastrophic drift under any failure condition
  • Zero catastrophic schema divergence under any failure condition
  • Zero catastrophic systemic-risk cascades under any failure condition
  • Full recovery to autonomous governance with complete reconciliation

docs.title: Infinite-Horizon Governance - Immortal Metadata - Perpetual Schema Cycles
docs.text:
The Eternity Book defines the infinite-horizon governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes how governance persists beyond temporal limits, how metadata becomes immortal, how schema cycles become perpetual, and how systemic-risk patterns stabilize across infinite time horizons.

This is the governance OS in its time-independent, immortal, perpetual form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Eternity Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • Infinite-horizon governance
  • Immortal metadata
  • Perpetual schema cycles
  • Eternal drift equilibrium
  • Time-independent systemic-risk models
  • Infinite-scale provenance
  • Eternal governance invariants

This book describes governance when time is no longer a constraint.

docs.title: 2.1 The Eternal Horizon
docs.text: Now -> Later -> Much Later -> Epochs -> Ages -> Infinity

docs.title: 2.2 Eternal Layers
docs.text:
1. Perpetual Layer - Governance that never expires.
  2. Immortal Layer - Metadata that never decays.
  3. Cyclic Layer - Schema that evolves in perpetual cycles.
  4. Invariant Layer - Governance rules that remain stable across infinite time.
  5. Asymptotic Layer - Governance that approaches perfect stability.

docs.title: 3.1 Metadata Without Decay
docs.text:
In the Eternity model:

  • Accuracy does not decay
  • Completeness does not decay
  • Relevance does not decay
  • Provenance does not decay

Metadata becomes time-independent.

docs.title: 3.2 Eternal Metadata Equation
docs.text: M(t) = M_0 (metadata remains constant across infinite time)

docs.title: 3.3 Eternal Provenance
docs.text: Provenance chains become infinite, immutable, quantum-safe, and time-anchored.

docs.title: 4.1 Schema as a Perpetual System
docs.text: Schema evolves in cycles: Birth -> Growth -> Maturity -> Renewal -> Birth -> ...

docs.title: 4.2 Schema Cycle Equation
docs.text: Schema(t + T) = Schema(t) where T = schema cycle period.

docs.title: 4.3 Eternal Schema Stability
docs.text: Schema cycles never fragment, never diverge, and never decay.

docs.title: 5.1 Drift in Infinite Time
docs.text: Drift does not accumulate. Drift does not decay. Drift reaches eternal equilibrium.

docs.title: 5.2 Drift Equilibrium Equation
docs.text: Drift_eq = limit as t -> infinity of Drift(t)

docs.title: 5.3 Eternal Drift Properties
docs.text:
• Always bounded
  • Always stable
  • Always correctable

docs.title: 6.1 Risk Over Infinite Time
docs.text: Risk becomes predictable, cyclic, and bounded.

docs.title: 6.2 Eternal Risk Equation
docs.text: Risk(t) = Risk_eq

docs.title: 6.3 Eternal Risk Properties
docs.text:
• No catastrophic cascades
  • No long-wave instability
  • No systemic collapse

docs.title: 7.1 Automation Without Aging
docs.text: Automation becomes ageless, perfectly aligned, and perfectly stable.

docs.title: 7.2 Eternal Automation Cycle
docs.text: Automation cycles become perpetual: Validate -> Execute -> Verify -> Renew -> Validate -> ...

docs.title: 7.3 Eternal Automation Equation
docs.text: Automation(t) = Automation* (stable attractor)

docs.title: 8.1 Eternal Provenance Graph
docs.text: The provenance graph becomes infinite: Node_0 -> Node_1 -> Node_2 -> ... -> Node_infinity

docs.title: 8.2 Eternal Provenance Properties
docs.text:
• Immutable
  • Complete
  • Infinite
  • Perfectly traceable

docs.title: 9.1 The Eternal Invariant
docs.text:
Governance converges to a single invariant:

Governance = Stability + Correctness + Provenance + Constraint

docs.title: 9.2 Invariant Properties
docs.text: The invariant is time-independent, drift-independent, schema-independent, and risk-independent.

docs.title: 9.3 The Eternal Horizon
docs.text: Governance approaches perfect stability: Stability(t) -> 1

docs.title: 10. Eternity Outcomes
docs.text:
• Infinite-horizon governance
  • Immortal metadata
  • Perpetual schema cycles
  • Eternal drift equilibrium
  • Time-independent systemic-risk models
  • Eternal provenance
  • Governance stability across infinite time

docs.title: UIAO Governance OS Executive Briefing Deck
Slide 1: Title
docs.text:
UIAO Governance OS

The Enterprise Governance Operating System

docs.title: Slide 2: What UIAO Is
docs.text:
UIAO is a governance operating system:

  • A governance engine that continuously detects, predicts, enforces, and controls governance state
  • A machine-trackable ontology where every document has provenance, ownership, and schema alignment
  • A federated truth fabric that unifies metadata, automation, schema, and systemic-risk governance
  • A platform where governance is automated, not manual

docs.title: Slide 3: Why UIAO Exists
docs.text:
The enterprise faces four compounding governance risks:

  • Metadata drift: documents silently diverge from canonical state
  • Schema fragmentation: multiple incompatible schema versions coexist
  • Automation instability: CI and workflow failures create blind spots
  • Systemic governance failure: local failures cascade into enterprise-wide risk

UIAO addresses all four, simultaneously and continuously.

docs.title: Slide 4: Governance OS Architecture
docs.text:
Five canonical layers form the governance stack:

Layer 1: Runtime - CI, Webhook, API, DB, Dashboards
Layer 2: Detection - Drift, SLA, Systemic-Risk
Layer 3: Prediction - Propagation Models, Simulators, RL Model
Layer 4: Enforcement - Normalization, SLA, Automation Alignment
Layer 5: Governance Control Plane - Schema, Automation, Owner, Systemic-Risk

docs.title: Slide 5: Runtime Layer
docs.text:
The foundation. All governance events originate here.

CI Validator -> Webhook Handler -> Governance API -> PostgreSQL -> Dashboards

Zero silent failures. Every event is captured, stored, and surfaced.

docs.title: Slide 6: Detection Layer
docs.text:
Three detection engines running continuously:

  • Drift Detection: identifies field-level, structural, and semantic drift
  • SLA Detection: monitors breach velocity and at-risk density
  • Systemic-Risk Detection: aggregates signals into a real-time risk score

Detection latency: under 5 minutes for automation events, real-time for drift events.

docs.title: Slide 7: Prediction Layer
docs.text:
Four predictive models:

  • Drift Propagation Model: predicts how detected drift will spread
  • Systemic-Risk Engine: forecasts systemic failure probability
  • Schema Evolution Simulator: models impact of schema changes before deployment
  • RL Governance Model: adaptive governance through reinforcement learning

Prediction enables proactive intervention, not reactive firefighting.

docs.title: Slide 8: Enforcement Layer
docs.text:
Four enforcement mechanisms:

  • Normalization Engine: automated fixes, remediation PRs, governance interventions
  • Intervention Ladder: four-level escalation from auto-fix to merge freeze
  • SLA Enforcement: timer management, breach escalation, owner notification
  • Automation Alignment: CI validator, workflow, and webhook patching

All enforcement actions are traceable to detection events.

docs.title: Slide 9: Governance Control Plane
docs.text:
Four governance domains:

  • Schema Governance: version pinning, deprecation management, migration
  • Automation Governance: CI, workflow, webhook configuration
  • Owner Governance: assignment, reliability tracking, escalation
  • Systemic-Risk Governance: threshold management, intervention triggers, merge freezes

The control plane orchestrates the entire governance OS.

docs.title: Slide 10: Systemic-Risk Governance
docs.text:
The systemic-risk domain provides three capabilities:

  • Early-Warning: real-time risk score with threshold alerting
  • Propagation: models how local failures cascade across the corpus
  • Diagnosis: root-cause analysis for systemic events

The propagation simulator enables stress-testing before production incidents occur.

docs.title: Slide 11: Drift Governance
docs.text:
Drift governance operates at four levels:

  • Detection: CI validator and weekly workflow catch drift within hours
  • Classification: four severity levels (Critical, High, Medium, Low)
  • Containment: playbook-driven response for each severity
  • Simulation: Monte-Carlo and propagation models for drift forecasting

The Drift-Resilience Index (DG-RI) measures corpus-level drift resistance.

docs.title: Slide 12: Metadata Reliability
docs.text:
The Metadata Governance Reliability Index (MG-RI) is the primary governance health indicator.

MG-RI aggregates: metadata quality, drift frequency, drift severity, SLA compliance, automation stability, and owner reliability.

Reliability heatmaps and scorecards provide visual surfaces for identifying gaps by owner and appendix.

docs.title: Slide 13: Schema and Normalization
docs.text:
Schema governance prevents the leading cause of metadata drift:

  • Schema Evolution Simulator models changes before deployment
  • Normalization Enforcement Engine detects and remediates violations automatically
  • Normalization Integrity Score (0-100) measures corpus-wide health

Below 60 triggers a normalization emergency with merge freeze.

docs.title: Slide 14: Runtime Resilience
docs.text:
The governance runtime is validated continuously:

  • Chaos-Engineering Test Plan: structured failure injection across all runtime components
  • Runtime Resilience Index (GR-RI): composite resilience score
  • Runtime Resilience Heatmap: component-by-factor resilience visualization
  • Observability Map: full coverage of runtime monitoring

docs.title: Slide 15: SLA and Owner Governance
docs.text:
SLA governance ensures accountability:

  • Every drift event creates an SLA timer
  • SLA breaches trigger escalation through the Intervention Ladder
  • Owner Reliability Score tracks 30-day performance trends
  • Reliability Scorecards surface owner-level accountability gaps

docs.title: Slide 16: Governance Outcomes
docs.text:
UIAO delivers six canonical governance outcomes:

  • Zero silent failures: all events are captured and surfaced
  • Zero schema drift: normalization engine enforces schema alignment continuously
  • Zero systemic drift clusters: propagation models enable early containment
  • Predictive governance: prediction layer forecasts failures before they occur
  • Autonomous normalization: low-severity violations are resolved automatically
  • Continuous runtime resilience: chaos engineering validates resilience continuously

docs.title: Slide 17: Strategic Value
docs.text:
UIAO delivers strategic enterprise value across four dimensions:

  • Enterprise stability: governance failures are contained before they become crises
  • Compliance alignment: provenance-aligned documents satisfy audit requirements
  • Operational resilience: chaos-validated runtime with automated recovery
  • Reduced systemic risk: propagation modeling and early intervention prevent cascades

docs.title: Slide 18: Closing
docs.text:
UIAO is the governance OS for the modern enterprise.

Deterministic. Schema-first. Provenance-aligned. Drift-resistant. Automation-enforced. Systemic-risk aware. Owner-accountable. Machine-trackable.

For full reference: uiao-governance-blue-book.md

For architecture: governance/governance-os-architecture.md

docs.title: Schema Standards - Metadata Standards - Normalization Standards
docs.text: The Green Book defines the canonical standards for schema, metadata, and corpus normalization across UIAO.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To establish:

  • Canonical schema rules
  • Metadata field standards
  • Normalization requirements
  • Schema evolution rules
  • Metadata quality thresholds

docs.title: 2.1 Required Fields
docs.text:
Every governance document must contain:

  • id
  • owner
  • appendix
  • status
  • classification
  • description
  • schemaVersion

docs.title: 2.2 Field Ordering
docs.text:
Fields must appear in canonical order:

  1. Metadata identity (id, title)
  2. Ownership (owner, appendix)
  3. Classification (status, classification)
  4. Description
  5. Schema metadata (schemaVersion)
  6. Operational metadata (created, updated)

docs.title: 2.3 Deprecated Fields
docs.text:
• Deprecated fields must be removed within one schema version cycle
  • CI validator must flag deprecated fields on every commit
  • Normalization engine must auto-remove deprecated fields for Low severity violations

docs.title: 3.1 Status Values
docs.text:
Canonical status values are:

  • current: document is active and in good standing
  • at-risk: document has open drift or SLA concerns
  • deprecated: document is scheduled for removal
  • archived: document is retained for historical reference only

docs.title: 3.2 Classification Values
docs.text:
Canonical classification values are:

  • core: foundational document required by governance charter
  • supporting: document that augments a core artifact
  • auxiliary: supplemental reference, not required for governance operations

docs.title: 3.3 Description Standards
docs.text:
• Must be written in complete sentences
  • Must be provenance-aligned (references canonical terms only)
  • Must be accurate to document content at time of commit

docs.title: 4.1 Structural Normalization
docs.text:
• All required fields must be present
  • All deprecated fields must be removed
  • Canonical field ordering must be enforced

docs.title: 4.2 Referential Normalization
docs.text:
• All cross-document ID references must resolve
  • No orphan references permitted
  • All referenced documents must exist in the corpus

docs.title: 4.3 Ownership Normalization
docs.text:
• Owner must be an active, recognized domain steward
  • Owner must be the correct steward for the document's domain
  • Inactive owners trigger reassignment workflow

docs.title: 4.4 Semantic Normalization
docs.text:
• Status values must be from the canonical set
  • Classification values must be from the canonical set
  • Descriptions must not reference deprecated or non-canonical terms

docs.title: 5.1 Versioning
docs.text:
• UIAO uses semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)
  • Breaking changes require a migration plan before rollout
  • Non-breaking additions require schema steward approval

docs.title: 5.2 Evolution Workflow
docs.text:
Propose -> Simulate -> Validate -> Approve -> Rollout

Each stage requires sign-off from the schema steward. The Schema Evolution Simulator must be run before Approve.

docs.title: 5.3 Schema Divergence Threshold
docs.text:
