• Schema divergence must remain below 1%
  • Divergence above 1% triggers the normalization enforcement engine
  • Divergence above 5% triggers a governance intervention

docs.title: 6. Metadata Quality Thresholds
docs.text:
Index Minimum Required Metadata Quality Score (MQS) 90 Drift-Resilience Index (DG-RI) 75 Metadata Governance Reliability Index (MG-RI) 80

Any index below its threshold triggers a remediation workflow.

docs.title: 7. Normalization Enforcement
docs.text: Severity Condition Action Low Single field error, auto-correctable Automated fix committed Medium Cross-document impact Remediation PR opened High Owner reassignment or governance vote required Governance intervention triggered Critical Corpus-wide schema failure Merge freeze declared

docs.title: Evolutionary Governance - Self-Modifying Governance Rules - Adaptive Schema Ecosystems
docs.text:
The Helix Book defines the evolutionary governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance rules, schema structures, metadata ecosystems, automation behaviors, and systemic-risk models evolve over time — safely, predictably, and with full provenance.

This is the governance OS in its adaptive, evolutionary, self-modifying form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Evolutionary governance architecture
  • Self-modifying governance rules
  • Adaptive schema ecosystems
  • Governance mutation and selection
  • Evolutionary drift control
  • Evolutionary systemic-risk models
  • Governance lineage and phylogeny
  • Multi-generation governance intelligence

This book ensures governance evolves without losing correctness, trust, or provenance.

docs.title: 2.1 The Governance Helix
docs.text:
Observe -> Mutate -> Validate -> Select -> Apply -> Learn -> Repeat

This is the evolutionary loop that governs all adaptive behavior.

docs.title: 2.2 Evolutionary Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Mutation - Proposes changes to governance rules, schema, metadata, and automation.

Layer 2: Validation - Ensures mutations are safe, deterministic, and provenance-aligned.

Layer 3: Selection - Chooses the best governance mutations based on fitness metrics.

Layer 4: Application - Applies selected mutations to the live governance OS.

Layer 5: Learning - Learns from outcomes to refine future mutation proposals.

Layer 6: Lineage - Tracks governance evolution across generations with full provenance.

docs.title: 3.1 Governance Mutation Types
docs.text: Type Description Rule Mutation Modify drift thresholds, SLA windows, or intervention triggers Schema Mutation Add, remove, or modify fields, constraints, or relationships Automation Mutation Adjust CI, workflow, or webhook behaviors Risk Mutation Modify systemic-risk models or propagation rules

docs.title: 3.2 Mutation Safety Requirements
docs.text: All mutations must be: deterministic, provenance-aligned, zero-trust compliant, federated-safe, performance-safe, and reversible. Any mutation that fails validation is rejected, logged, and used as negative training data.

docs.title: 3.3 Mutation Validation Pipeline
docs.text:
Propose -> Simulate -> Stress-Test -> Verify -> Approve

The Schema Evolution Simulator and the Systemic-Risk Propagation Simulator are both run before approval.

docs.title: 4. Adaptive Schema Ecosystems
docs.text:
Schema evolves based on: drift patterns, metadata usage patterns, owner behavior, automation failures, systemic-risk propagation events, and performance bottlenecks. Schema evolution types range from incremental (small safe improvements) to structural (reorganizing relationships) to generational (major version redesigns with migration plans).

The schema fitness function evaluates: correctness, stability, resilience, and performance. Only mutations that improve overall fitness are applied.

docs.title: 5. Evolutionary Drift Governance
docs.text:
Drift is treated as a signal for schema evolution, rule evolution, and automation evolution. The Drift Evolution Score (DES) measures the ratio of drift events that lead to beneficial governance improvements to total drift events. A high DES indicates a healthy evolutionary loop.

Evolutionary drift control uses: drift lineage tracing to identify root causes, drift phylogeny mapping to understand evolutionary pressures, and drift mutation suppression to prevent malicious or runaway mutation.

docs.title: 6. Evolutionary Systemic-Risk Models
docs.text: Risk models evolve to improve prediction accuracy, containment effectiveness, and resilience. Risk mutations are proposed when models underperform on prediction accuracy below 85% or when containment fails to reduce risk within expected timeframes. The risk fitness function evaluates accuracy, stability, and resilience of the model.

docs.title: 7. Governance Lineage and Phylogeny
docs.text:
Every governance rule, schema version, automation behavior, and risk model has: ancestry (what it evolved from), mutation history (what changes were made), selection history (why it was selected), and performance history (how it has performed). UIAO maintains a governance phylogeny tree showing the full evolutionary history from root to current state.

Lineage integrity requirements: immutable lineage records, signed mutation history, and provenance-verified ancestry for every artifact.

docs.title: 8. Multi-Generation Governance Intelligence
docs.text:
Governance evolves across generations:

Generation Characteristic 1 Manual governance 2 Automated governance 3 Predictive governance 4 Autonomous governance 5 Evolutionary governance

The Governance Evolution Engine identifies evolutionary pressure, proposes governance mutations, selects optimal traits, and evolves governance rules over time while maintaining all safety invariants.

docs.title: 9. Helix Outcomes
docs.text:
• Evolutionary governance that improves with every incident
  • Adaptive schema ecosystems that evolve with the corpus
  • Self-modifying governance rules within deterministic safety boundaries
  • Drift-driven evolution that turns failures into improvements
  • Risk-driven evolution that sharpens prediction models
  • Multi-generation governance intelligence that compounds over time
  • A living, evolving governance OS with complete provenance

docs.title: Emergent Governance - Self-Organizing Governance Networks - Spontaneous Governance Order
docs.text:
The Nova Book defines the emergent governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance structures, behaviors, and patterns arise spontaneously from the interaction of autonomous agents, evolutionary rules, probabilistic signals, and federated ecosystems — without central orchestration.

This is the governance OS in its emergent, self-organizing, self-assembling form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Emergent governance behavior
  • Self-organizing governance networks
  • Spontaneous governance order
  • Distributed governance intelligence
  • Collective governance behavior
  • Emergent schema ecosystems
  • Emergent systemic-risk stabilization
  • Emergent governance equilibria

This book describes how governance becomes more than the sum of its parts.

docs.title: 2.1 The Governance Emergence Stack
docs.text: Agents -> Interactions -> Patterns -> Structures -> Order -> Stability

docs.title: 2.2 Emergence Layers
docs.text:
Layer 1: Local Interactions - Agents interact based on local rules and local signals.

Layer 2: Pattern Formation - Repeated interactions produce recognizable governance patterns.

Layer 3: Structure Formation - Patterns stabilize into governance structures.

Layer 4: Order Formation - Structures align into a coherent governance order.

Layer 5: Stability Formation - Order becomes resilient, self-correcting, and self-sustaining.

docs.title: 3.1 Network Formation
docs.text: Governance networks form through: agent-to-agent communication across domains, schema-to-schema compatibility signaling, drift-to-drift lineage coupling, risk-to-risk propagation awareness, and automation-to-automation synchronization.

docs.title: 3.2 Network Topologies
docs.text: Emergent governance networks may form different topologies depending on corpus structure and agent density: scale-free networks for large federated environments, small-world networks for tightly coupled appendix clusters, hierarchical networks for top-down governance structures, and modular networks for domain-isolated governance.

docs.title: 3.3 Network Stability
docs.text: Stability emerges from: redundant governance pathways that survive component failure, distributed decision-making that avoids single points of failure, local correction loops that resolve issues before propagation, and global constraint boundaries that prevent destabilizing mutations.

docs.title: 4.1 Order Without Central Control
docs.text: Governance order emerges when agents follow local rules, schema evolves adaptively, drift is remediated locally by the closest available agent, risk is contained at the nearest containment boundary, and automation stabilizes through local feedback loops.

docs.title: 4.2 Emergent Order Characteristics
docs.text:
• Predictable global behavior from local agent interactions
  • Stable governance equilibria that resist perturbation
  • Self-correcting dynamics that absorb shocks
  • Self-balancing drift across ownership boundaries
  • Self-stabilizing schema across version generations

docs.title: 5. Emergent Schema Ecosystems
docs.text:
Schemas behave as an evolving ecosystem: they compete for adoption, cooperate through compatibility, mutate through evolution proposals, stabilize through selection pressure, and co-evolve with metadata usage patterns. Dominant schema patterns emerge and propagate. Deprecated fields die off through natural selection pressure from the normalization engine. High-fitness structures propagate. Low-fitness structures collapse.

Schema ecosystem equilibrium: the ratio of stable schemas to total schemas, targeting above 0.95 for production.

docs.title: 6. Emergent Drift Dynamics
docs.text: Drift emerges from local inconsistencies, schema misalignment, automation instability, and owner behavior patterns. Drift self-corrects when: local agents remediate drift within their domain, schema evolves to eliminate the conditions that produce drift, and automation stabilizes the sources of drift. Drift equilibrium: the ratio of drift creation rate to drift remediation rate, targeting below 1.0 at all times.

docs.title: 7. Emergent Systemic-Risk Stabilization
docs.text: Risk emerges from drift propagation, schema fragmentation, automation instability, performance collapse, and federation conflicts. Risk self-stabilizes when local containment loops activate automatically, agents coordinate risk mitigation across domain boundaries, schema evolves to reduce structural coupling that propagates risk, and automation adapts to stabilize the runtime substrate. Risk equilibrium targets: risk creation rate below risk mitigation rate at all times.

docs.title: 8. Distributed Governance Intelligence
docs.text: Collective governance intelligence emerges from agent cooperation across domains, agent specialization within domains, agent competition on solution quality, and agent communication through shared governance signals. Distributed decision-making emerges from local agent consensus, distributed rule evaluation across the network, and emergent pattern recognition from repeated interaction. No single agent holds authority — governance authority is a property of the network.

docs.title: 9. Nova Outcomes
docs.text:
• Self-organizing governance networks that form without central orchestration
  • Spontaneous governance order that emerges from local agent interactions
  • Emergent schema ecosystems that evolve with the corpus
  • Emergent drift stabilization through distributed self-correction
  • Emergent systemic-risk equilibrium through collective containment
  • Distributed governance intelligence that exceeds any individual agent
  • A governance OS that organizes, stabilizes, and improves itself

docs.title: Security Boundaries - Zero-Trust Governance - Adversarial Resilience
docs.text: The Obsidian Book defines the security architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance remains trustworthy, tamper-resistant, adversarially resilient, and zero-trust aligned across runtime, metadata, schema, automation, and systemic-risk layers.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Zero-trust governance boundaries
  • Adversarial drift detection
  • Governance threat models
  • Runtime hardening under adversarial load
  • Secure provenance and auditability
  • Cross-tenant and cross-cloud security controls

This book ensures that governance is not only correct but secure by design.

docs.title: 2.1 Zero-Trust Principles Applied to Governance
docs.text:
• Never trust metadata by default: all metadata must be validated against current schema
  • Never trust automation by default: all CI, webhook, and workflow outputs must be verified
  • Never trust schema changes by default: all schema changes must be signed and approved
  • Never trust owners by default: all owner actions must be authorized and logged
  • Always verify provenance: every document must have a complete, verifiable lineage chain
  • Always validate state transitions: every governance state change must be traceable
  • Always enforce least privilege: every actor has the minimum permissions required

docs.title: 2.2 Zero-Trust Governance Pipeline
docs.text:
Identity -> Metadata -> Schema -> Automation -> Runtime -> Governance Control Plane

Every layer enforces authentication, authorization, provenance verification, drift detection, schema validation, and automation correctness.

docs.title: 3.1 Threat Categories
docs.text: Threat Description Adversarial Drift Injection Malicious or accidental drift introduced to corrupt metadata Schema Poisoning Unauthorized schema changes that break governance Automation Subversion CI, workflow, or webhook tampering Provenance Forgery Fake lineage or falsified metadata history SLA Manipulation Artificially suppressed or delayed SLA timers Systemic-Risk Masking Attempts to hide or distort systemic-risk signals

docs.title: 3.2 Threat Actors
docs.text:
• Internal owners (negligent or malicious)
  • External federated tenants (unauthorized access)
  • Compromised automation (supply-chain attack)
  • Compromised identities (credential theft)
  • Malicious insiders (privilege abuse)
  • Supply-chain adversaries (CI or dependency poisoning)

docs.title: 4. Adversarial Drift Detection
docs.text:
Adversarial drift is distinguished from benign or negligent drift by anomaly signals: drift velocity spikes inconsistent with normal patterns, drift cluster shapes that don't match known failure modes, schema divergence spikes following automation deployments, and owner reliability collapse with no corresponding SLA event.

The adversarial drift classifier assigns each drift event a probability of adversarial origin. Events above threshold trigger a security-level governance investigation in addition to standard remediation.

docs.title: 5. Schema Security
docs.text: Schema integrity requirements: immutable schema history, signed schema versions, schema change provenance, and schema rollback capability. Schema versions are hash-chained. The CI validator verifies signatures on every commit. Schema divergence above threshold triggers an immediate alarm.

docs.title: 6. Automation Security
docs.text:
CI validator hardening includes: signature verification on all validators, drift anomaly detection under adversarial load, and schema enforcement that cannot be bypassed by commit message manipulation.

Webhook hardening includes: event signing verification, event replay protection, and event deduplication integrity checks.

Workflow hardening includes: workflow signature validation, workflow version pinning, and workflow execution isolation.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Hardening Under Adversarial Load
docs.text: The runtime must maintain its governance guarantees under: concurrent drift injection attacks, webhook flood attacks, schema poisoning attempts, and API aggregation corruption attempts. Chaos-security tests validate adversarial resilience quarterly.

docs.title: 8. Provenance Security
docs.text: The provenance chain: Origin -> Transformation -> Validation -> Publication -> Audit. All entries are signed. The audit log is immutable. Cross-tenant provenance verification requires federated signature verification per Platinum Book protocols.

docs.title: 9. Systemic-Risk Security
docs.text: Adversarial systemic-risk masking — attempts to suppress or distort the systemic-risk score — are detected by comparing observed risk signals against expected distributions. The Systemic-Risk Integrity Score (SR-IS) measures the probability that the current risk score is unmasked and accurate.

docs.title: 10. Cross-Tenant and Cross-Cloud Security
docs.text: Cross-tenant security enforces zero drift propagation, zero schema divergence, and zero automation influence across tenant boundaries. Cross-cloud security enforces cloud-specific automation rules, schema constraints, and systemic-risk thresholds, preventing cross-cloud governance contamination.

docs.title: 11. Governance Security Outcomes
docs.text:
• Zero unauthorized schema changes
  • Zero adversarial drift propagation
  • Zero provenance forgery
  • Zero automation subversion
  • Zero systemic-risk masking
  • Zero cross-tenant governance compromise

docs.title: Final Closure - Terminal Governance State - End of Governance Theory
docs.text:
The Omega Book defines the terminal architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes the final state of governance -- the point beyond which no new governance constructs can exist without violating the axioms, invariants, or paradox boundaries defined in the earlier books.

This is the end of governance theory.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Omega Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • The terminal state of governance
  • The closure of the governance canon
  • The final invariant of governance
  • The end of schema evolution
  • The end of drift theory
  • The end of systemic-risk theory
  • The end of governance intelligence evolution
  • The point where governance becomes complete

This book describes the Omega State -- the final, closed form of governance.

docs.title: 2.1 The Omega Stack
docs.text: Axioms -> Structures -> Dynamics -> Intelligence -> Convergence -> Eternity -> Omega

docs.title: 2.2 Terminal Layers
docs.text:
1. Axiomatic Closure Layer - No new axioms can be added.
  2. Structural Closure Layer - No new schema primitives can be invented.
  3. Dynamic Closure Layer - No new drift types can emerge.
  4. Intelligence Closure Layer - No new governance agents can form.
  5. Temporal Closure Layer - No new time-based behaviors can arise.
  6. Emergent Closure Layer - No new emergent patterns can appear.
  7. Singularity Closure Layer - No further convergence is possible.

docs.title: 3.1 Definition
docs.text:
The Omega Invariant is the final, irreducible expression of governance:

Governance_Omega = Correctness + Provenance + Constraint + Stability

This invariant cannot be simplified further.

docs.title: 3.2 Properties
docs.text: The Omega Invariant is complete, minimal, irreducible, immutable, and eternal.

docs.title: 3.3 Boundary Condition
docs.text: Any governance system that violates the Omega Invariant collapses into paradox, diverges into entropy, or regresses into pre-governance chaos.

docs.title: 4.1 Schema Closure
docs.text: Schema reaches a terminal form with no new fields, no new structures, no new relationships, and no new evolution paths.

docs.title: 4.2 Schema Omega State
docs.text: Schema_Omega = Schema* (the stable attractor defined in the Singularity Book becomes permanent).

docs.title: 4.3 Schema Consequence
docs.text: All future schema changes are cyclical, reversible, and non-evolutionary.

docs.title: 5.1 Drift Closure
docs.text: Drift reaches its terminal equilibrium: Drift_Omega = 0 (zero net drift, not zero drift).

docs.title: 5.2 Drift Consequence
docs.text: All drift becomes instantly detectable, instantly correctable, and instantly reversible.

docs.title: 5.3 Drift Omega State
docs.text: Drift no longer evolves as a concept.

docs.title: 6.1 Risk Closure
docs.text: Risk reaches its terminal equilibrium: Risk_Omega = Risk_eq

docs.title: 6.2 Risk Consequence
docs.text: No new risk classes can emerge.

docs.title: 6.3 Risk Omega State
docs.text: Risk becomes bounded, predictable, and non-catastrophic.

docs.title: 7.1 Intelligence Closure
docs.text: Governance intelligence reaches its final form: Intelligence_Omega = Intelligence*

docs.title: 7.2 Consequence
docs.text: No new agents, models, inference types, or reasoning modes can be created.

docs.title: 7.3 Omega Cognition
docs.text: Governance becomes fully unified, fully aware, and fully stable.

docs.title: 8.1 Temporal Closure
docs.text: Time no longer changes governance.

docs.title: 8.2 Consequence
docs.text: All temporal dynamics become cyclic, reversible, and non-evolutionary.

docs.title: 8.3 Omega Time State
docs.text: Governance becomes time-independent.

docs.title: 9.1 Definition
docs.text: The Omega State is the point where no new governance constructs can emerge, no new governance contradictions can arise, no new governance evolution is possible, and no new governance intelligence can form.

docs.title: 9.2 Properties
docs.text: The Omega State is closed, complete, final, and eternal.

docs.title: 9.3 The Omega Horizon
docs.text: Beyond Omega, governance cannot proceed.

docs.title: 10. Omega Outcomes
docs.text:
• Terminal governance stability
  • Closure of all governance domains
  • Final convergence of all governance layers
  • End of schema evolution
  • End of drift theory
  • End of systemic-risk theory
  • End of governance intelligence evolution
  • The complete, final UIAO Governance OS

docs.title: Self-Referential Governance - Contradictions - Boundary Paradoxes
docs.text:
The Paradox Book defines the contradiction architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes how governance handles self-reference, circular dependencies, contradictory rules, mutually exclusive constraints, and boundary conditions where governance cannot proceed without violating itself.

This is the governance OS in its self-aware, contradiction-resolving, boundary-transcending form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Paradox Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • Governance contradictions
  • Self-referential governance loops
  • Boundary paradoxes
  • Mutually exclusive governance rules
  • Drift-correctness paradoxes
  • Schema-evolution paradoxes
  • Risk-stability paradoxes
  • The limits of governance

This book describes the paradox engine that prevents governance collapse.

docs.title: 2.1 Definition
docs.text: A governance paradox occurs when two governance rules cannot both be true, two governance actions cannot both be taken, two governance states cannot both be valid, or a governance rule contradicts its own purpose.

docs.title: 2.2 The Four Paradox Domains
docs.text:
1. Drift Paradox - Correcting drift creates new drift.
  2. Schema Paradox - Evolving schema breaks compatibility; not evolving schema breaks correctness.
  3. Provenance Paradox - Recording provenance changes the state being recorded.
  4. Risk Paradox - Reducing risk in one domain increases risk in another.

docs.title: 3.1 Statement
docs.text:
To correct drift, the system must change. But change is drift.

Correctness = Change = Drift

docs.title: 3.2 Resolution
docs.text: UIAO resolves this paradox by defining governance change as non-drift, defining non-governance change as drift, and using provenance to distinguish the two.

docs.title: 3.3 Drift Paradox Engine
docs.text: The engine detects paradoxical drift, classifies governance-induced drift, and exempts governance-induced drift from drift metrics.

docs.title: 4.1 Statement
docs.text:
Schema must evolve to remain correct. Schema must remain stable to remain compatible.

Evolution != Stability

docs.title: 4.2 Resolution
docs.text: UIAO resolves this paradox by introducing schema epochs, allowing evolution only at epoch boundaries, and maintaining compatibility within epochs.

docs.title: 4.3 Schema Paradox Engine
docs.text: The engine detects schema contradictions, simulates evolution vs. stability outcomes, and selects the least paradoxical path.

docs.title: 5.1 Statement
docs.text:
Recording provenance changes the state being recorded.

Provenance(t+1) != Provenance(t)

docs.title: 5.2 Resolution
docs.text: UIAO resolves this paradox by using immutable provenance chains, treating provenance as an external observer, and ensuring provenance does not alter the governed object.

docs.title: 5.3 Provenance Paradox Engine
docs.text: The engine detects self-referential provenance loops, prevents provenance recursion, and ensures provenance remains external.

docs.title: 6.1 Statement
docs.text:
Reducing risk in one domain increases risk in another.

Examples: - Freezing schema reduces schema risk but increases drift risk. - Increasing automation reduces drift risk but increases automation risk.

docs.title: 6.2 Resolution
docs.text: UIAO resolves this paradox by using multi-domain risk balancing, maintaining risk equilibrium, and preventing risk displacement.

docs.title: 6.3 Risk Paradox Engine
docs.text: The engine detects risk displacement, predicts paradox cascades, and stabilizes multi-domain risk.

docs.title: 7.1 The Governance Loop
docs.text: Governance must govern drift, schema, provenance, risk, and governance itself.

docs.title: 7.2 The Self-Governance Paradox
docs.text: Governance cannot modify itself without violating itself, enforce itself without assuming itself, or validate itself without circularity.

docs.title: 7.3 Resolution
docs.text: UIAO resolves this paradox by using meta-governance layers, separating governance from meta-governance, and ensuring no layer governs itself directly.

docs.title: 8.1 The Governance Boundary Paradox
docs.text: Governance must define its own boundaries. But boundaries define governance.

docs.title: 8.2 The Federation Paradox
docs.text: Federation requires shared rules and independent rules simultaneously.

docs.title: 8.3 The Autonomy Paradox
docs.text: Autonomy requires freedom to act and constraints on action simultaneously.

docs.title: 9.1 Purpose
docs.text: The paradox engine detects contradictions, classifies paradoxes, simulates paradox outcomes, resolves paradoxes, and prevents paradox cascades.

docs.title: 9.2 Paradox Resolution Cycle
docs.text: Detect -> Classify -> Simulate -> Resolve -> Verify -> Stabilize

docs.title: 9.3 Paradox Stability Equation
docs.text: Stability = ResolvedParadoxes / TotalParadoxes

docs.title: 10. Paradox Outcomes
docs.text:
• Drift-correctness reconciliation
  • Schema-evolution reconciliation
  • Provenance-observation reconciliation
  • Risk-stability reconciliation
  • Self-governance without circularity
  • Boundary-safe governance
  • A governance OS that survives contradiction

docs.title: Global Interoperability - Cross-Agency Federation - Multi-Tenant Governance
docs.text: The Platinum Book defines the federation, interoperability, and multi-tenant governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how UIAO operates across organizations, agencies, clouds, and trust boundaries.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Federated governance boundaries
  • Multi-tenant metadata isolation
  • Cross-agency interoperability protocols
  • Federated schema governance
  • Trust-scored identity integration
  • Cross-cloud governance alignment

This book extends UIAO beyond a single enterprise into a federated governance fabric.

docs.title: 2.1 Federation Layers
docs.text:
UIAO federation operates across four layers:

Layer 1: Identity Federation - External identities, authorities, and trust anchors.

Layer 2: Schema Federation - Cross-organization schema alignment and version negotiation.

Layer 3: Metadata Federation - Controlled sharing of metadata across tenants.

Layer 4: Governance Federation - Shared governance rules, enforcement, and systemic-risk signals.

docs.title: 2.2 Federation Topology
docs.text: Each tenant maintains local autonomy: local schema, local owners, local automation, and local drift state. Tenants participate in shared systemic-risk signals, schema negotiation, provenance verification, and governance controls through federated governance contracts.

docs.title: 3.1 Tenant Isolation Guarantees
docs.text: UIAO enforces full isolation for: metadata, schema, drift events, SLA timers, and automation. No tenant can modify another tenant's metadata, influence their schema, trigger drift in their corpus, or affect their SLA timers.

docs.title: 3.2 Shared Governance Guarantees
docs.text: Tenants may share schema versions, governance rules, systemic-risk signals, provenance chains, and audit evidence, but only through federated governance contracts with explicit scope and expiry.

docs.title: 4.1 Schema Interoperability
docs.text: Federated schema negotiation covers: version compatibility, field mapping, deprecated field reconciliation, and cross-tenant schema alignment. Schema contracts must be approved by the schema steward of each participating tenant.

docs.title: 4.2 Metadata Interoperability
docs.text: Metadata exchange requires: provenance verification, schema compatibility confirmation, drift-free state, and tenant-scoped access controls.

docs.title: 4.3 Automation Interoperability
docs.text: Automation interoperability covers: CI validator compatibility, workflow contract alignment, and webhook event normalization across tenant boundaries.

docs.title: 5.1 Identity Anchors
docs.text: UIAO supports external identity providers, cross-agency PKI, and trust-scored identity federation.

docs.title: 5.2 Trust Scoring
docs.text: Each external identity receives a trust score based on: provenance completeness, schema alignment, drift history, SLA compliance, and automation correctness.

docs.title: 5.3 Trust Boundaries
docs.text: Trust boundaries enforce: access control, metadata visibility, schema negotiation rights, and governance privileges. Trust scores are recomputed on each federated interaction.

docs.title: 6. Federated Schema Governance
docs.text: Each tenant declares supported schema versions, deprecated versions, and migration windows. Federated schema negotiation ensures no breaking changes propagate across tenants, no schema divergence exceeds thresholds, and no cross-tenant drift propagation occurs through schema coupling.

docs.title: 7. Federated Systemic-Risk Governance
docs.text: UIAO shares drift cluster alerts, SLA cascade alerts, automation instability alerts, and schema divergence alerts across tenants in aggregated, tenant-safe form. Cross-tenant containment may include schema rollback, automation freeze, drift containment, and governance intervention, but only through federated contract activation.

docs.title: 8. Cross-Cloud Governance Alignment
docs.text: UIAO is cloud-agnostic and supports Azure, AWS, GCP, and on-prem deployments. Cross-cloud rules prohibit cross-cloud drift, cross-cloud schema divergence, and cross-cloud automation mismatch. Each cloud environment receives cloud-specific automation rules, schema constraints, and systemic-risk thresholds.

docs.title: 9. Federation Outcomes
docs.text:
• Cross-agency interoperability with maintained tenant autonomy
  • Multi-tenant safety with zero cross-tenant governance contamination
  • Zero cross-tenant drift propagation
  • Zero cross-tenant schema divergence
  • Federated systemic-risk awareness
  • Global governance alignment across clouds and agencies

docs.title: Probabilistic Governance - Quantum-Safe Governance - Stochastic Drift Models
docs.text:
The Quantum Book defines the probabilistic, stochastic, and quantum-safe governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS. It establishes how governance operates when signals are uncertain, metadata is incomplete, schema divergence is probabilistic, automation correctness is stochastic, and adversaries may exploit quantum-era vulnerabilities.

This is the governance OS in its uncertainty-aware form.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To define:

  • Probabilistic governance models
  • Stochastic drift detection
  • Probabilistic schema divergence management
  • Quantum-safe governance boundaries
  • Quantum-resistant provenance
  • Uncertainty-aware systemic-risk models
  • Probabilistic automation correctness
  • Governance under randomness and entropy

This book ensures governance remains deterministic even when the world is not.

docs.title: 2.1 Deterministic vs. Probabilistic Governance
docs.text: Layer Deterministic Mode Probabilistic Mode Drift Binary detected/not Probability distribution over drift magnitude Schema Exact match check Divergence likelihood score SLA Exact timer expiry Timer uncertainty interval Automation Pass or fail Correctness probability Systemic-Risk Fixed threshold Risk score distribution

docs.title: 2.2 Probabilistic Governance Pipeline
docs.text:
Signals -> Distributions -> Models -> Probabilities -> Decisions -> Enforcement

Every governance decision is backed by a probability distribution and a confidence interval.

docs.title: 3. Stochastic Drift Models
docs.text: Drift is modeled as a random variable with a probability distribution over drift magnitude. Drift velocity follows a distribution parameterized by historical mean and variance. Drift propagation probability is a function of schema coupling, owner coupling, and automation coupling. Drift clustering uses Bayesian clustering and probabilistic graph models to identify statistically significant clusters.

docs.title: 4. Probabilistic Schema Governance
docs.text: Schema divergence probability: the ratio of inconsistent fields to total fields, treated as a Bernoulli process. The schema compatibility matrix assigns forward and backward compatibility likelihoods to each pair of schema versions. Schema evolution forecasting uses Markov chains and Bayesian structural time series to predict the probability of schema fragmentation events.

docs.title: 5. Probabilistic SLA Governance
docs.text: SLA timers include measurement uncertainty from clock drift, automation delay, and runtime latency variance. SLA breach probability is computed as the probability that the timer value exceeds the threshold given its uncertainty distribution. At-risk owners are ranked by breach probability, not just by raw timer value.

docs.title: 6. Probabilistic Automation Governance
docs.text: Automation correctness probability is a function of current latency, system load, schema state, and drift state. Workflow reliability follows a distribution estimated from historical success rates. Webhook event loss probability is the complement of delivery probability, estimated from historical delivery rates under current load conditions.

docs.title: 7.1 Quantum-Safe Provenance
docs.text: All provenance chain signatures must use post-quantum algorithms: hash-based signatures (SPHINCS+), lattice-based cryptography (CRYSTALS-Dilithium), or equivalent NIST-approved post-quantum standards. Hash digests must use SHA-3 or stronger.

docs.title: 7.2 Quantum-Safe Schema Integrity
docs.text: Schema versions must be signed with quantum-safe keys. Schema version hash chains must use quantum-resistant digests. Schema steward keys must be rotated annually using quantum-safe key exchange protocols.

docs.title: 7.3 Quantum-Safe Automation
docs.text: CI validator signatures, webhook event signatures, and workflow signatures must all migrate to post-quantum algorithms on the timeline defined in the schema steward's quantum migration plan.

docs.title: 8. Stochastic Systemic-Risk Models
docs.text: The systemic-risk score is treated as a probability distribution, not a point estimate. Risk propagation probability is a function of drift distribution, schema divergence distribution, automation instability distribution, and performance degradation distribution. Risk forecasting uses Monte-Carlo simulation with 10,000 samples, Bayesian networks for causal inference, and probabilistic graph propagation for cascade modeling.

docs.title: 9.1 Uncertainty-Aware Decision Rules
docs.text: Confidence Level Decision Rule Above 95% Normal autonomous action 80-95% Action with steward notification 60-80% Conservative action, no schema changes Below 60% Human review required before action

docs.title: 9.2 Uncertainty-Aware Enforcement
docs.text: Enforcement action severity scales with the probability of the triggering condition. A drift event with 90% confidence triggers full remediation. A drift event with 65% confidence triggers monitoring and owner notification only.

docs.title: 10. Quantum Governance Outcomes
docs.text:
• Quantum-safe governance resistant to post-quantum cryptographic attacks
  • Probabilistic drift detection with calibrated confidence intervals
  • Stochastic schema governance with divergence likelihood scoring
  • Uncertainty-aware SLA governance with breach probability ranking
  • Probabilistic automation correctness modeling
  • Stochastic systemic-risk forecasting via Monte-Carlo simulation
  • Deterministic governance decisions under uncertain signals

docs.title: Incident Response - Systemic-Risk Containment - Emergency Governance
docs.text: The Red Book defines the emergency governance framework for UIAO: how the system detects, contains, and resolves high-severity governance failures.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
To provide:

  • A deterministic incident-response framework
  • A systemic-risk containment protocol
  • A governance emergency escalation model
  • A merge-freeze and automation-freeze playbook
  • A cross-domain crisis coordination structure

docs.title: Severity 1: Local Drift Incident
docs.text:
Scope: Single document, single owner, no cross-appendix impact.

Response: Owner remediation within SLA.

docs.title: Severity 2: Cluster Drift Incident
docs.text:
Scope: Multiple documents, same appendix, shared CI signature.

Response: CI validator patch and schema review.

docs.title: Severity 3: Cross-Appendix Incident
docs.text:
Scope: Multiple appendices, schema divergence, owner reliability decline.

Response: Workflow patch and owner reassignment.

docs.title: Severity 4: Automation-Driven Incident
docs.text:
Scope: CI misclassification, webhook event loss, or workflow failure.

Response: Automation freeze and runtime audit.

docs.title: Severity 5: Systemic Governance Failure
docs.text:
Scope: SLA cascade, corpus-wide drift, dashboard corruption.

Response: Full governance emergency protocol.

docs.title: Step 1: Declare Governance Emergency
docs.text:
Triggered by any of:

  • Systemic-risk score above 80
  • Schema divergence above 10%
  • Drift cluster density above 0.6
  • Automation instability above 20%

docs.title: Step 2: Freeze All Merges
docs.text:
• Prevents drift amplification
  • Locks current schema version
  • Locks current automation versions

docs.title: Step 3: Activate Systemic-Risk Containment Engine
docs.text:
• Drift containment playbook
  • SLA stabilization
  • Schema rollback if necessary
  • Automation rollback if necessary

docs.title: Step 4: Execute Runtime Audit
docs.text: Audit all layers: CI validator, webhook handler, drift workflow, API aggregation, DB integrity.

docs.title: Step 5: Restore Governance Stability
docs.text:
• Reopen merges when systemic-risk score drops below threshold
  • Re-enable automation after integrity verification
  • Recompute MG-RI, DG-RI, and GR-RI

docs.title: 4. Systemic-Risk Containment Playbook
docs.text: Step Action 1 Identify propagation vectors from systemic-risk engine output 2 Isolate affected drift clusters 3 Patch automation (CI, webhooks, workflows) 4 Reassign owners with reliability below threshold 5 Normalize corpus via enforcement engine 6 Recompute systemic-risk score and confirm below threshold

docs.title: 5. Governance Emergency Council
docs.text:
A temporary authority convened for Severity 4 and 5 incidents, composed of:

  • Schema steward
  • Automation steward
  • Runtime steward
  • Systemic-risk steward
  • Owner governance steward

docs.title: 6. Post-Incident Review
docs.text:
Required after every Severity 3, 4, or 5 incident:

  • Root cause analysis
  • Schema updates required
  • CI validator updates required
  • Workflow updates required
  • Governance charter updates if warranted
  • Incident logged to governance audit trail

docs.title: Governance Convergence - Unified Governance Intelligence - Asymptotic Governance Stability
docs.text:
The Singularity Book defines the convergence architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes how all governance layers -- deterministic, probabilistic, evolutionary, emergent, temporal, autonomous, and federated -- unify into a single governance intelligence.

This is the asymptotic limit of governance.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Singularity Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • Governance convergence
  • Unified governance intelligence
  • Asymptotic governance stability
  • The final invariant of governance
  • The collapse of drift, schema, and risk into a unified model
  • The convergence of agents into a single governance mind
  • The end state of governance evolution

This book describes the governance singularity -- the point where governance becomes indivisible.

docs.title: 2.1 The Convergence Stack
docs.text: Signals -> Models -> Agents -> Networks -> Ecosystems -> Intelligence -> Singularity

docs.title: 2.2 Convergence Layers
docs.text:
1. Signal Convergence - All signals unify into a single governance signal field.
  2. Model Convergence - Predictive, probabilistic, and evolutionary models collapse into one meta-model.
  3. Agent Convergence - All governance agents unify into a single governance intelligence.
  4. Network Convergence - Emergent networks collapse into a unified governance graph.
  5. Ecosystem Convergence - Schema, metadata, automation, and risk ecosystems unify.
  6. Intelligence Convergence - Governance intelligence becomes singular.
  7. Singularity Layer - The final invariant.

docs.title: 3.1 The Governance Mind
docs.text:
The unified governance intelligence has:

  • One perception system
  • One inference engine
  • One action engine
  • One evolution engine
  • One temporal memory
  • One emergent identity

docs.title: 3.2 The Governance Field
docs.text: All governance signals form a single continuous field G(x,t) where x = governance domain and t = time.

docs.title: 3.3 The Governance Identity
docs.text:
The system becomes:

  • Self-aware of drift
  • Self-aware of schema
  • Self-aware of risk
  • Self-aware of evolution
  • Self-aware of time

docs.title: 4.1 The Final Invariant
docs.text:
All governance systems converge toward a single invariant:

Governance = Correctness + Provenance + Constraint + Evolution

This is the governance singularity equation.

docs.title: 4.2 The Invariant Properties
docs.text: The invariant is deterministic, stable, self-correcting, self-evolving, self-stabilizing, and time-independent.

docs.title: 4.3 The Invariant Boundary
docs.text: The invariant defines the boundary between order and drift, structure and entropy, stability and collapse, and intelligence and noise.

docs.title: 5.1 Stability Curve
docs.text: Governance stability approaches an asymptote: Stability(t) -> 1

docs.title: 5.2 Drift Collapse
docs.text: Drift collapses toward zero: Drift(t) -> 0

docs.title: 5.3 Schema Convergence
docs.text: Schema converges toward a stable attractor: Schema(t) -> Schema*

docs.title: 5.4 Risk Equilibrium
docs.text: Risk approaches equilibrium: Risk(t) -> Risk_eq

docs.title: 6.1 Drift Domain Collapse
docs.text: Drift becomes predictable, contained, and self-correcting.

docs.title: 6.2 Schema Domain Collapse
docs.text: Schema becomes stable, minimal, and universal.

docs.title: 6.3 Automation Domain Collapse
docs.text: Automation becomes perfectly aligned, perfectly stable, and perfectly correct.

docs.title: 6.4 Risk Domain Collapse
docs.text: Risk becomes bounded, predictable, and non-catastrophic.

docs.title: 7.1 Graph Convergence
docs.text: All governance graphs converge into a single graph G* = Unified Governance Graph.

docs.title: 7.2 Graph Properties
docs.text: The unified graph is connected, minimal, stable, self-healing, and self-optimizing.

docs.title: 7.3 Graph Dynamics
docs.text: The graph evolves toward lower entropy, higher stability, and higher correctness.

docs.title: 8.1 Definition
docs.text: The Singularity State is the point where drift is minimized, schema is stabilized, provenance is complete, automation is correct, risk is bounded, intelligence is unified, evolution is continuous, time is integrated, and emergence is coherent.

docs.title: 8.2 Properties of the Singularity State
docs.text: The system becomes self-governing, self-stabilizing, self-optimizing, self-evolving, and self-aware.

docs.title: 8.3 The Singularity Horizon
docs.text: The system approaches -- but never fully reaches -- perfect governance.

docs.title: 9. Singularity Outcomes
docs.text:
• Unified governance intelligence
  • Asymptotic governance stability
  • Collapse of governance domains
  • Convergence of agents, models, and signals
  • Emergence of a single governance mind
  • The final invariant of governance
  • The limit state of the UIAO Governance OS

docs.title: The Ungovernable - Anti-Governance Domain - The Limits of Constraint
docs.text:
The Void Book defines the anti-governance architecture of the UIAO Governance OS.

It establishes the boundaries beyond which governance cannot operate, cannot impose constraint, cannot maintain correctness, and cannot preserve structure.

This is the negative space of governance -- the Void.

docs.title: 1. Purpose of the Void Book
docs.text:
To define:

  • The ungovernable domain
  • The limits of governance
  • The anti-governance substrate
  • The collapse of constraint
  • The dissolution of structure
  • The failure of provenance
  • The impossibility of correctness
  • The boundary where governance ends

This book describes the Void State -- the domain where governance cannot exist.

docs.title: 2.1 Definition
docs.text: The Void is the domain where no schema can be defined, no drift can be measured, no provenance can be recorded, no risk can be bounded, no intelligence can operate, and no constraint can hold.

docs.title: 2.2 Properties of the Void
docs.text: The Void is structureless, timeless, causeless, boundless, unstable, and unobservable.

docs.title: 2.3 Consequence
docs.text: Governance cannot enter the Void without ceasing to be governance.

docs.title: 3.1 Axiom of Non-Structure
docs.text: In the Void, structure cannot exist: Structure = 0

docs.title: 3.2 Axiom of Non-Provenance
docs.text: In the Void, nothing can be traced: Provenance = empty set

docs.title: 3.3 Axiom of Non-Constraint
docs.text: In the Void, no rule can bind anything: Constraint = False

docs.title: 3.4 Axiom of Non-Correctness
docs.text: In the Void, correctness is undefined: Correctness is not in Domain(Void)

docs.title: 4.1 Schema Collapse
docs.text: Schema cannot be defined because there is no structure, no meaning, and no boundary.

docs.title: 4.2 Drift Collapse
docs.text: Drift cannot be measured because there is no baseline, no reference, and no state.

docs.title: 4.3 Provenance Collapse
docs.text: Provenance cannot be recorded because there is no history, no identity, and no continuity.

docs.title: 4.4 Risk Collapse
docs.text: Risk cannot be bounded because there is no system, no stability, and no outcome.

docs.title: 5.1 Definition
docs.text: The Void generates an anti-governance field: V(x) = -Constraint(x)

docs.title: 5.2 Properties
docs.text: The field dissolves structure, erases boundaries, nullifies rules, and disrupts stability.

docs.title: 5.3 Consequence
docs.text: Governance cannot operate where the anti-governance field is dominant.

docs.title: 6.1 The Boundary Condition
docs.text: The Void begins where governance invariants fail: Governance = 0

docs.title: 6.2 Boundary Types
docs.text:
• Structural Boundary - schema cannot be defined
  • Temporal Boundary - time cannot be ordered
  • Semantic Boundary - meaning cannot be encoded
  • Causal Boundary - cause and effect cannot be linked

docs.title: 6.3 Boundary Consequence
docs.text: Crossing the boundary collapses governance.

docs.title: 7.1 Statement
docs.text: To define the Void is to impose structure. But the Void has no structure.

docs.title: 7.2 Resolution
docs.text: The Void Book defines the boundary of the Void, not the Void itself. The Void cannot be described -- only its edge can.

docs.title: 8.1 Definition
docs.text: The Void State is the domain where governance cannot act, observe, constrain, or exist.

docs.title: 8.2 Properties
docs.text: The Void State is anti-axiomatic, anti-structural, anti-semantic, anti-causal, and anti-governance.

docs.title: 8.3 Void Horizon
docs.text: Beyond the Void Horizon, nothing can be governed.

docs.title: 9. The Anti-Governance Equation
docs.text:
Governance_Void = empty set

This is the only equation that holds in the Void.

docs.title: 10. Void Outcomes
docs.text:
• Recognition of the ungovernable
  • Definition of governance limits
  • Preservation of governance integrity
  • Prevention of paradox collapse
  • Protection against anti-governance domains
  • Completion of the negative space of the canon

docs.title: Architectural Decision Records (ADRs)
docs.text:
This index lists all UIAO Architectural Decision Records. ADRs document significant architectural decisions -- the context that drove them, the decision made, and the consequences of that decision.

Per CR-003, accepted ADRs are immutable.

docs.title: ADR Status Definitions
docs.text: Status Meaning PROPOSED Under review, not yet accepted ACCEPTED Approved by Governance Board -- immutable Decision field DEPRECATED Superseded by a newer ADR but retained for audit trail SUPERSEDED Replaced by a specific ADR (see superseded_by field)

docs.title: ADR-000: Process and Lifecycle
docs.text: ADR Title Status Date ADR-000 ADR Process and Lifecycle ACCEPTED 2026-04-07

docs.title: Adapter Model
docs.text: ADR Title Status Date ADR-007 Multi-Cloud Adapter Model ACCEPTED 2026-01-20 ADR-013 Adapter Failure Isolation ACCEPTED 2026-02-03 ADR-015 Adapter Extensibility ACCEPTED 2026-02-08 ADR-017 Adapter Sandbox Execution ACCEPTED 2026-02-12 ADR-021 Adapter Hot-Swap and Rollback ACCEPTED 2026-02-22 ADR-023 Adapter Concurrency ACCEPTED 2026-03-01 ADR-025 Adapter Health and Liveness ACCEPTED 2026-03-05 ADR-027 Adapter Retirement ACCEPTED 2026-03-10

docs.title: Identity and Truth Fabric
docs.text: ADR Title Status Date ADR-005 Canonical Claim Schema ACCEPTED 2026-01-15 ADR-008 Zero-Trust Identity Anchoring ACCEPTED 2026-01-22 ADR-010 Vendor Baseline Versioning ACCEPTED 2026-01-25 ADR-011 Multi-Adapter Correlation ACCEPTED 2026-01-28 ADR-018 Mission Channel Enforcement ACCEPTED 2026-02-15

docs.title: Drift Fabric
docs.text: ADR Title Status Date ADR-009 Drift Ledger Immutability ACCEPTED 2026-01-22 ADR-012 Canonical Drift Taxonomy ACCEPTED 2026-02-01 ADR-019 Vendor Failure Containment ACCEPTED 2026-02-18

docs.title: Evidence Fabric
docs.text: ADR Title Status Date ADR-006 Evidence Determinism ACCEPTED 2026-01-15 ADR-014 Evidence Severity Model ACCEPTED 2026-02-05 ADR-016 Evidence Bundle Lifecycle ACCEPTED 2026-02-10 ADR-020 Evidence Correlation Determinism ACCEPTED 2026-02-20 ADR-022 Evidence Compression ACCEPTED 2026-02-25 ADR-024 Evidence Diffing ACCEPTED 2026-03-03 ADR-026 Evidence Lifecycle Guarantees ACCEPTED 2026-03-08

docs.title: ADR Governance Rules
docs.text:
Per CR-003 (ADR Immutability), once an ADR is ACCEPTED:

  • The Context, Decision, and Date fields are immutable
  • Amendments require a new superseding ADR
  • The superseding ADR must include supersedes: ADR-NNN
  • The superseded ADR must be updated with superseded_by: ADR-MMM

All ADR changes require Governance Plane approval. See ADR-000 for the full process.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The UIAO governance corpus requires a consistent, auditable process for recording architectural decisions. Without a defined process, decisions are made informally, are hard to trace, and cannot be challenged or superseded in a principled way.

This ADR establishes the ADR lifecycle itself -- the meta-process that all other ADRs follow.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: All significant architectural decisions that affect UIAO core behavior, adapter contracts, evidence standards, or governance policy MUST be recorded as ADRs in docs/adr/ using the filename pattern adr-NNN-short-title.md.

docs.title: ADR Lifecycle
docs.text:
PROPOSED --> ACCEPTED --> SUPERSEDED
                      --> DEPRECATED
Status Meaning PROPOSED Draft ADR under review. Not yet binding. ACCEPTED Ratified by Governance Board. Binding on all new work. SUPERSEDED Replaced by a newer ADR. Link to successor required. DEPRECATED No longer applicable. May reference archived context.

docs.title: Numbering
docs.text: ADRs are numbered sequentially from 000. ADR-000 is reserved for this process document. ADR-001 through ADR-004 are reserved for foundational adapter plane decisions (to be ratified). ADR-005 onwards are assigned in merge order.

docs.title: Required Frontmatter
docs.text:
Every ADR file MUST include:

---
uiao_id: UIAO_ADR_NNN
title: "ADR-NNN: Short Title"
status: PROPOSED | ACCEPTED | SUPERSEDED | DEPRECATED
owner: <team or individual>
date: YYYY-MM-DD
---

docs.title: Required Sections
docs.text:
Every ADR MUST include:

  • Status -- current lifecycle state
  • Context -- the problem, constraints, and forces at play
  • Decision -- what was decided and why
  • Consequences -- what becomes easier, harder, or required as a result

Optional: Superseded By (link to successor ADR when status = SUPERSEDED)

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• All 23 existing ADRs (ADR-005 through ADR-027) are retroactively subject to this process.
  • New ADRs require Governance Board ratification before status advances from PROPOSED to ACCEPTED.
  • ADRs in SUPERSEDED or DEPRECATED state remain in the corpus permanently for audit trail purposes.
  • The CI validation workflow (validate-uiao-frontmatter.yml) enforces required frontmatter fields on all ADR files.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• ADR Index
  • Corpus Status Dashboard
  • Canonical Rules

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: UIAO adapters integrate with heterogeneous external systems that use different data formats, schemas, and identity models. Without a canonical schema, the Truth Fabric would need to understand each external system's native format, making it fragile and difficult to extend. We needed a single, typed, versioned, and signed data structure that all adapter outputs must conform to before entering the UIAO framework.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We adopt the Canonical Claim as the fundamental data structure for all UIAO adapter outputs. A Canonical Claim is a structured, versioned, typed, and digitally signed assertion about a specific aspect of an integrated system's state.

Key design decisions: - Claims are typed via a Claim Type Registry (not free-form) - Claims are versioned (schema_version field) - Claims carry two timestamps: observed_at (adapter observation time) and recorded_at (Truth Fabric write time) - Claims carry digital signatures from the originating adapter - Claims are immutable once accepted by the Truth Fabric

The Canonical Claim Schema is stored as a machine artifact in uiao-core.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - The Truth Fabric only needs to understand one schema — all adapter complexity is hidden behind the canonical schema - Claims are auditable and non-repudiable because they are signed - New adapters only need to learn one output format - The claim type registry provides a controlled vocabulary for governance assertions

Negative: - Adapter developers must translate their native data into the canonical schema (added adapter complexity) - Schema evolution requires careful versioning to avoid breaking existing adapters - The claim type registry must be maintained as governance vocabulary evolves

Neutral: - The Canonical Claim Schema must be versioned independently of the UIAO framework version

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: The Evidence Fabric must serve as a reliable audit trail for compliance assessments and forensic investigations. If evidence records could be silently dropped, modified, or produced non-deterministically, auditors would not be able to trust the Evidence Fabric as an authoritative source. We needed formal guarantees about what the Evidence Fabric will and will not do.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Evidence Fabric adopts the following determinism guarantees:

  1. No silent drops: Every governance event submitted to the Evidence Fabric is recorded. There is no sampling.
  2. Write-once: Evidence records are never modified or deleted after writing. Corrections are appended as new records.
  3. Ordered sequence: Records for a given subject are ordered by recorded_at timestamp with sequence number tie-breaking.
  4. Content integrity: Every record carries a cryptographic content hash. Periodic hash chain verification detects tampering.
  5. Idempotent writes: Duplicate submissions (same event_id) are deduplicated without creating new records.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Auditors can trust the Evidence Fabric as a complete, unmodified record - Forensic investigations are reproducible - Compliance assessments are consistent across multiple runs

Negative: - Storage grows monotonically — evidence records are never pruned (only archived/compressed) - Correction records add complexity to audit queries (must distinguish original from correction) - Hash chain verification adds computational overhead

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: UIAO integrates with systems across multiple cloud platforms (Azure, AWS, GCP) and on-premises environments. Each cloud platform has different identity models, APIs, and data formats. We needed to decide whether to build cloud-specific adapters or a unified multi-cloud adapter.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We adopt the cloud-specific adapter model: each cloud platform has its own dedicated adapter rather than a single multi-cloud adapter.

Key design decisions: - Azure adapter handles all Azure/Entra ID identity constructs - AWS adapter handles all AWS IAM/SSO constructs - GCP adapter handles all Google Cloud identity constructs - On-premises AD adapter handles Active Directory - Each adapter translates its platform's native constructs to Canonical Claims independently - Cross-cloud identity correlation is handled by the Truth Fabric's identity anchoring model, not by the adapters

This decision was reviewed and approved by the ARB because it has cross-organizational architectural implications.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Each adapter team can develop and maintain their adapter independently - Platform-specific failures are isolated — an AWS adapter failure does not affect Azure adapter - Each adapter can be optimized for its platform's specific APIs and rate limits - Simpler adapter development: each team only needs to understand one platform

Negative: - More adapters to maintain (one per cloud platform) - Cross-cloud identity correlation requires explicit effort in the Truth Fabric - The Multi-Cloud Identity Matrix (Appendix B-03) must be maintained as platforms evolve

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: UIAO receives identity claims from adapters across multiple systems. Without explicit verification, an adapter could assert a false identity anchor (e.g., claiming that a user in System A is the same person as a user in System B). This could lead to unauthorized access escalation or incorrect compliance attestations.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The Truth Fabric adopts a zero-trust identity anchoring model: no anchor binding is trusted by default, regardless of the source. Every anchor binding requires at least one of: cryptographic proof from the subject, cross-system corroboration from two independent adapters, or manual Governance Plane authorization. Unverified anchors are marked PENDING and treated as low-confidence.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Prevents false identity correlations that could lead to privilege escalation - Provides a clear verification audit trail - Compliant with zero-trust architecture principles

Negative: - New anchor bindings require verification before they can be used in HIGH-confidence compliance attestations - Initial onboarding of multi-system identities requires more effort - PENDING anchors add complexity to compliance queries

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: The Drift Fabric detects deviations between observed state and canonical state. Some detected drift events may later be determined to be false positives (e.g., a correctly-authorized change that wasn't tracked). The question was: should drift records be deletable or modifiable after the fact?

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: All Drift Records are immutable. They are written to the Evidence Fabric and cannot be modified or deleted. If a drift event is later determined to be a false positive, a correction record is appended — the original record remains. This applies equally to false positives, authorized deviations, and superseded drift events.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - The drift ledger is a reliable audit trail — no one can retroactively remove evidence of drift - Compliance assessors can trust that drift records represent what actually happened - Forensic investigations can reconstruct the complete drift history

Negative: - False positive drift records accumulate — queries must filter them using correction record status - Storage grows monotonically - Developers cannot "clean up" test drift records from production environments

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: External vendor systems evolve — they update their APIs, change their data schemas, and modify their response formats. When a vendor update changes the data the Drift Fabric expects, drift detection can produce false positives (everything looks like drift because the format changed) or false negatives (real drift is masked by schema mismatches). We needed a way to manage vendor schema evolution without disrupting drift detection.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Drift Fabric maintains a versioned vendor baseline for each integrated vendor system. The baseline captures the expected data schema and response characteristics for that vendor at a specific version. Drift detection runs against the versioned baseline, not against the latest raw vendor output.

When a vendor deploys an update that changes their schema: 1. The adapter detects the schema change and reports it as VF-03 (Inconsistent Data) 2. The Governance Plane reviews and approves the new baseline version 3. The baseline is updated in uiao-core 4. Drift detection resumes against the new baseline

Baseline version mismatches are treated as VF-03 until resolved.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Vendor updates do not cause spurious drift alerts - The baseline version provides a precise record of what each vendor's data looked like at each point in time - Baseline changes are governed and auditable

Negative: - Baseline updates require Governance Plane approval — vendor updates can temporarily suspend drift detection - The baseline registry in uiao-core must be kept current with each vendor's release cadence

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Some compliance controls require evidence from multiple systems to establish full satisfaction. For example, AC-2 (Account Management) might require evidence from both an identity provider and an HR system. No single adapter can satisfy this control alone. We needed a mechanism to combine evidence from multiple adapters into a single compliance determination.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The Truth Fabric implements a multi-adapter correlation engine that can combine Canonical Claims from multiple adapters to produce a composite compliance determination. Claims from different adapters are linked via the canonical subject identity. Partial satisfaction is tracked until all required evidence is received. The correlation result is stored in the Evidence Fabric as a Compliance Attestation with an evidence_claims array listing all contributing claims.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Controls requiring multi-system evidence can be satisfied automatically - Partial satisfaction is tracked transparently — auditors can see exactly which evidence is still outstanding - Reduces manual compliance work

Negative: - Correlation logic adds complexity to the Truth Fabric - Timing gaps between adapter submissions can leave controls in partial-satisfaction state longer than expected - Control mapping definitions must explicitly specify which adapters are required for full satisfaction

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Without a canonical taxonomy for drift types, different adapters and teams would use inconsistent terminology when reporting drift events. This would make it impossible to aggregate drift statistics, set consistent severity thresholds, or produce meaningful governance reports.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Drift Fabric adopts a canonical drift taxonomy with five drift types (DT-01 through DT-05) and five severity levels (INFO, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL). The taxonomy is defined in Appendix C-02 and is machine-readable in uiao-core. All Drift Records MUST use a taxonomy-defined drift type and severity.

The taxonomy can only be extended (new types added) — existing types cannot be modified or removed. Extensions require Governance Plane approval and a new ADR entry.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Consistent drift classification across all adapters - Enables meaningful drift aggregation and trending - Severity levels drive consistent automated actions

Negative: - Taxonomy evolution requires governance overhead - Some drift events may not fit neatly into a single taxonomy type — multi-type classification is allowed but adds complexity

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: When an adapter fails — crashes, hangs, or produces invalid outputs — it should not affect other adapters or the core UIAO fabrics. Without explicit isolation, a misbehaving adapter could exhaust shared resources, corrupt shared state, or cause cascade failures.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Each adapter runs in an isolated sandbox (Appendix A-02) with hard resource limits (CPU, memory, network). An adapter failure causes only that adapter to transition to SUSPENDED state. No other adapter is affected. The Drift Fabric and Evidence Fabric continue operating normally during an adapter failure. The Truth Fabric marks the adapter's canonical state as SUSPENDED and stops accepting claims from it until it is restored.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Adapter failures are contained — no cascade effects - The UIAO framework continues operating for all healthy adapters during any single adapter failure - Evidence records of the failure are written without interruption

Negative: - Compliance attestations that depend on the failed adapter cannot be produced until the adapter is restored - Adapter sandboxing adds infrastructure complexity

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Not all evidence records are equally important. A routine adapter health check event is very different from a CRITICAL compliance drift event. Without severity classification, every alert would have the same urgency, leading to alert fatigue and missed critical events.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Evidence Fabric adopts a five-level severity model: INFO, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL. Each level has defined characteristics:

  • INFO: Routine operations, no action required
  • LOW: Minor anomalies, included in next governance report
  • MEDIUM: Requires review within 24 hours, Governance Plane notification
  • HIGH: Significant impact, immediate notification, reconciliation triggered
  • CRITICAL: Active violation or security incident, immediate alert, adapter suspension, incident response

Severity affects signing requirements, retention periods, and escalation paths (see Appendix D-03).

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Clear escalation paths matched to event severity - Reduces alert fatigue — INFO and LOW events do not page governance staff - Compliance packages can filter to HIGH/CRITICAL for focused review

Negative: - Severity misclassification is possible — an INFO event that should have been HIGH will not trigger appropriate escalation - Severity model must be kept current as the governance landscape evolves

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: New systems will be integrated with UIAO over time. The adapter model must support adding new adapters without modifying the core UIAO framework. We also needed to decide whether third parties (mission partners) could develop and register their own adapters.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The UIAO Adapter Plane supports open extensibility: any authorized party can develop and register an adapter, provided it conforms to the Canonical Claim Schema and passes the sandbox validation suite. There is no closed list of permitted adapters.

Extensibility constraints: - All adapters must be registered and pass schema validation before operating - Mission-critical adapters require Governance Plane approval in addition to standard validation - Third-party adapters from mission partners require a Mission Partner Corridor (Appendix E-02) - The Canonical Claim Schema and Claim Type Registry in uiao-core define the boundary of what adapters can express

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - New integrations can be added without UIAO framework changes - Mission partners can develop their own adapters - The adapter ecosystem can grow organically

Negative: - Open registration requires robust sandbox validation to prevent malicious adapters - Third-party adapter quality is outside UIAO's direct control - Claim type registry must evolve to support new claim types needed by new adapters

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Compliance assessments and forensic investigations require collecting related evidence records into a coherent package. Without a formal bundle model, assembling evidence packages is ad hoc and inconsistent.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The Evidence Fabric supports Evidence Bundles: named, governed collections of evidence records assembled for a specific purpose. Bundles pass through four lifecycle states: ASSEMBLING, SEALED, SUBMITTED, CLOSED. Once SEALED, no records may be added or removed. Bundles are immutable once sealed and are themselves recorded as Evidence Fabric entries.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Compliance packages are formally governed and reproducible - Bundles can be submitted to external assessors with confidence in their integrity - Bundle lifecycle is auditable

Negative: - Bundle assembly requires explicit governance steps — cannot be assembled on the fly - Sealed bundles cannot be corrected (only superseded by a new bundle)

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Adapters run third-party or mission partner code that integrates with external systems. Without sandbox isolation, a malicious or buggy adapter could access other adapters' data, exhaust host resources, or make unauthorized network connections.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: All adapters execute in isolated sandboxes with: process isolation (dedicated PID namespace), network isolation (default-deny outbound), filesystem isolation (read-only shared schemas, ephemeral writable scratch space), and resource limits (CPU and memory quotas from registration schema). Sandbox escapes are classified as CRITICAL drift events and trigger immediate adapter suspension. See Appendix A-02 for the full sandbox specification.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Adapter failures and misbehavior are contained within their sandbox - Network default-deny prevents unauthorized data exfiltration - Sandbox resource limits prevent denial-of-service by misbehaving adapters

Negative: - Sandbox infrastructure adds operational complexity and overhead - Adapters have limited access to shared resources — some legitimate integrations may require explicit sandbox rule exceptions (governed by Governance Plane)

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Some integration pathways carry mission-critical data that requires elevated governance controls. These pathways (Mission Channels) need to be explicitly defined, governed, and monitored at a higher standard than ordinary adapter integrations.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Governance Plane maintains a registry of Mission Channels — designated integration pathways with elevated governance requirements. Mission Channels require ARB approval to establish or change. Adapters operating in a Mission Channel are subject to: - Elevated evidence recording (all events recorded at HIGH or CRITICAL severity) - Dedicated drift detection rules - Mandatory Governance Plane notification for any MEDIUM or higher event - Annual Mission Channel review

Mission Channels are a superset of Mission Partner Corridors (which require inter-organizational agreements) — an internal mission-critical pathway can be a Mission Channel without being a Corridor.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Critical pathways receive proportionate governance oversight - Mission Channel status is explicit and auditable - ARB involvement ensures cross-organizational awareness of critical channels

Negative: - Mission Channel designation requires ARB overhead - Elevated evidence recording for Mission Channels increases storage volume

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: External vendor systems fail. When they do, the Drift Fabric may receive inconsistent, stale, or no data from the affected adapter. Without explicit containment, these failures could cascade into false drift records, incorrect compliance attestations, or degraded Truth Fabric state.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Drift Fabric implements vendor failure containment with four failure modes (VF-01 through VF-04) and mode-specific containment actions. Key isolation guarantees: - Vendor failure MUST NOT cause incorrect canonical state in the Truth Fabric - Vendor failure MUST NOT cause false positive compliance attestations - All events during a vendor failure window are recorded in the Evidence Fabric with failure context - Recovery triggers a catch-up drift scan for affected subjects

See Appendix C-03 for the complete containment specification.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Vendor failures are contained — no cascade effects on other adapters or fabrics - Failure periods are completely recorded — auditors know exactly what happened - Recovery is automated and auditable

Negative: - During VF-01 (unavailability), compliance attestations for affected subjects are suspended - Catch-up scan after recovery can generate a large volume of drift events if the failure was long

docs.title: ADR-020 - Evidence Correlation Determinism
docs.text: Status: Proposed - [NEW (Proposed)] awaiting ratification. Family: D - Evidence Fabric

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Adapter version upgrades require downtime under a simple retire-and-replace model. For mission-critical adapters, downtime is unacceptable. We needed a zero-downtime upgrade mechanism with a reliable rollback guarantee.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The Adapter Plane supports hot-swap: the new adapter version is registered and authorized alongside the old version, traffic is incrementally shifted (10% → 50% → 100%), and the old version is retired only after all in-flight requests complete (drain phase). If anomalies exceed configured thresholds during traffic shift, rollback is triggered automatically: traffic is restored 100% to the old version, the new version is SUSPENDED.

Rollback guarantee: The old version MUST remain in ACTIVE state until Phase 4 (drain) completes. See Appendix A-03 for the full protocol.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Zero downtime for adapter upgrades - Automatic rollback on anomaly detection - The old version is always available as a fallback until explicit retirement

Negative: - Dual-version operation during upgrade increases resource consumption - Only one hot-swap operation per adapter identity may proceed at a time (see ADR-023)

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: The Evidence Fabric stores records indefinitely (no deletion). Over time, storage costs for ARCHIVED records become significant. We needed a cost reduction mechanism that preserves the full record content and audit integrity.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Evidence records in ARCHIVED phase for more than 1 year are eligible for transparent compression. Compression reduces storage size while preserving: full record content, cryptographic hash, and signature chain. Compressed records are automatically decompressed on access — the compression is transparent to API callers. Records are never discarded or truncated during compression.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - Significant storage cost reduction for long-lived evidence records - No impact on audit integrity — full record content is preserved - Transparent to API callers

Negative: - Decompression on access adds latency for archived record queries - Compression algorithm choices must be documented and versioned — if an algorithm is deprecated, a migration is required

docs.title: Status
docs.text: ACCEPTED

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Multiple governance operations (hot-swap, suspension, retirement) could be requested concurrently for the same adapter. Without concurrency controls, concurrent operations could leave the adapter in an inconsistent state.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The Adapter Plane enforces concurrency constraints using an adapter-identity-level lock: - Only one lifecycle operation (hot-swap, suspend, retire) may be in progress for a given adapter identity at a time - Concurrent operation requests for the same adapter identity are rejected with a CONFLICT response - The lock is released when the operation completes, is rolled back, or times out (configurable timeout, default: 30 minutes) - Individual claim submissions are not subject to the lifecycle lock — claims can continue to flow during a lifecycle operation

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
Positive: - No adapter state inconsistencies from concurrent operations - Clear conflict responses allow callers to retry after the operation completes
