docs.title: Canonical Operating Manual for Maintainers & Governance Stewards
1. Purpose
docs.text: This handbook provides a complete, printable reference for the UIAO Governance Runtime. It defines the governance model, roles, workflows, SLAs, runtime architecture, and operational procedures.

docs.title: 2. Governance Principles
docs.text:
• Metadata must be correct, complete, and schema-aligned.
  • Drift must be detected early and remediated quickly.
  • Owners are accountable for their documents.
  • Governance enforces SLAs and prevents systemic drift.
  • All actions must be deterministic, auditable, and provenance-preserving.

docs.title: 3. Roles
docs.text:
Maintainers: Remediate drift, open remediation PRs, ensure CI passes.

Appendix Owners: Acknowledge drift within 72 hours, remediate critical items within 14 days.

Governance Stewards: Enforce SLAs, review systemic drift, maintain governance rules and schema.

docs.title: 4. Drift Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Drift detected (CI or weekly workflow)
  2. Issue created with severity labels
  3. Owner acknowledgment (<=72h)
  4. Remediation PR opened
  5. CI validation
  6. Merge + resolution
  7. Weekly workflow confirms closure

docs.title: 5. SLAs
docs.text:
• Acknowledgment SLA: 72 hours
  • Remediation SLA (critical): 14 days
  • Escalation: sla-at-risk -> sla-breached -> governance review

docs.title: 6. CI Enforcement
docs.text: CI blocks merges on: missing required fields, deprecated fields, invalid IDs, broken references, YAML errors. Emergency bypass requires governance approval.

docs.title: 7. Governance Runtime Architecture
docs.text:
GitHub -> Webhook Handler -> Governance API -> Dashboard UI
                          -> PostgreSQL (state)

docs.title: 8. Dashboard
docs.text: Corpus Index, SLA Heatmap, Weekly Reports, Owner Reliability Scores.

docs.title: 9. Reliability Score
docs.text: Weighted formula combining: Time-to-Acknowledge, Time-to-Remediate, SLA breaches, CI failures, weekly unacknowledged items. See owner-reliability-score.md for full formula.

docs.title: 10. Quarterly Governance Review
docs.text: Drift trends, SLA performance, reliability scores, systemic drift clusters, schema updates, governance interventions. See quarterly-review-template.md for the template.

docs.title: 11. Deployment
docs.text:
• Local: docker compose up -d
  • Production: Helm chart
  • Reverse proxy: NGINX

See runtime-deploy.md for full deployment guide.

docs.title: 12. Related Documents
docs.text: Document Purpose bootstrap.md Full onboarding guide escalation-playbook.md SLA and escalation state machine governance-charter.md Formal governance policy steward-playbook.md Steward responsibilities day1-owner-quickstart.md New owner quick start runtime-architecture.md Runtime component overview quarterly-review-template.md Quarterly review format

docs.title: Canonical Escalation Levels for Drift, SLA Breaches, and Systemic Risk
docs.text: This ladder defines the five levels of governance intervention, from minor drift correction to full systemic governance action.

docs.title: Level 1 - Advisory
docs.text:
Trigger: - Low/medium drift - No SLA risk

Actions: - Owner notification - Standard remediation PR - No escalation

docs.title: Level 2 - Corrective
docs.text:
Trigger: - Repeated drift - SLA-at-risk - CI validator failures

Actions: - Owner coaching - Required remediation plan - Reliability score adjustment

docs.title: Level 3 - Escalation
docs.text:
Trigger: - SLA-breached - Critical drift unacknowledged - Owner unresponsive

Actions: - Governance steward intervention - Ownership reassignment - Mandatory training

docs.title: Level 4 - Systemic
docs.text:
Trigger: - Drift clusters across appendices - Schema divergence - Automation drift

Actions: - Schema update - CI validator update - Workflow update - Systemic drift RCA

docs.title: Level 5 - Governance Emergency
docs.text:
Trigger: - Widespread automation failure - SLA system collapse - Corpus-wide metadata corruption

Actions: - Freeze merges - Activate manual SLA enforcement - Full runtime audit - Executive notification

docs.title: UIAO Governance Maturity Progression Roadmap (L1 to L5)
Evolution Path from Ad-Hoc Governance to Autonomous Governance
docs.text: This roadmap defines the progression from minimal governance to fully autonomous, predictive governance.

docs.title: Level 1 - Ad-Hoc
docs.text:
• No schema enforcement
  • Drift discovered manually
  • No SLA model
  • No dashboard
  • No automation

Goal: Establish basic schema + CI validator.

docs.title: Level 2 - Structured
docs.text:
• Schema defined
  • Manual reviews
  • CI validator partially enforced
  • Drift tracked inconsistently
  • Owners assigned manually

Goal: Implement weekly drift workflow + SLA model.

docs.title: Level 3 - Automated Detection
docs.text:
• CI validator blocks merges
  • Weekly drift workflow operational
  • Owners assigned automatically
  • Drift consistently surfaced
  • Dashboard provides basic visibility

Goal: Add SLA enforcement + reliability scoring.

docs.title: Level 4 - SLA-Driven Governance
docs.text:
• SLA timers enforced
  • Escalations automated
  • Dashboard includes heatmaps + reliability trends
  • Governance reviews systemic drift
  • Schema evolution process formalized

Goal: Add predictive models + systemic drift clustering.

docs.title: Level 5 - Autonomous Governance
docs.text:
• Full runtime stack deployed
  • Predictive drift detection
  • Automated owner reassignment suggestions
  • Autonomous metadata normalization
  • Governance AI assistant
  • Systemic-risk forecasting

Goal: Maintain continuous governance optimization.

docs.title: Statistical + ML Hybrid Model for Detecting Unusual Drift Patterns
docs.text: This model identifies anomalous drift behavior that deviates from historical norms.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To detect: unexpected drift spikes, unusual severity distributions, cross-appendix anomalies, owner-specific anomalies, and automation-related anomalies.

docs.title: A. Drift Velocity (DV)
docs.text: Weekly drift count.

docs.title: B. Severity Distribution (SD)
docs.text: Critical/high/medium/low proportions.

docs.title: C. Cross-Appendix Correlation (CAC)
docs.text: Similarity of drift patterns across appendices.

docs.title: D. CI Error Signatures (CIES)
docs.text: Hash of validator error patterns.

docs.title: E. Owner Reliability (OR)
docs.text: 30-day reliability trend.

docs.title: 3. Statistical Baseline
docs.text:
An anomaly is flagged when:

|DV - mean_DV| > 2 * std_DV

or

|SD - mean_SD| > 2 * std_SD

or

CAC > mean_CAC + 2 * std_CAC

docs.title: Model Type
docs.text:
• Isolation Forest
  • One-Class SVM
  • Autoencoder reconstruction error

docs.title: Feature Vector
docs.text: X = [DV, SD, CAC, CIES, OR]

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
• Anomaly score (0-1)
  • Drift anomaly classification

docs.title: 5. Governance Actions
docs.text:
• Validate anomaly
  • Check CI validator
  • Check workflow logs
  • Check schema divergence
  • Trigger systemic drift review

docs.title: Canonical Actions for Stopping Drift Spread Across the Corpus
docs.text: This playbook defines the containment strategy for preventing local drift from escalating into systemic drift.

docs.title: 1. Containment Triggers
docs.text:
• Drift cluster forming within 7 days
  • Same CI signature across multiple documents
  • SLA pressure rising across multiple owners
  • Schema divergence > 5%
  • Automation anomalies detected

docs.title: Step 1 - Isolate the Drift
docs.text:
• Freeze related PRs
  • Flag affected documents
  • Notify owners

docs.title: Step 2 - Identify Drift Type
docs.text:
• Structural
  • Referential
  • Ownership
  • Semantic
  • Automation

docs.title: Structural
docs.text:
• Enforce schema validation
  • Patch CI validator

docs.title: Referential
docs.text:
• Validate all IDs
  • Repair cross-appendix references

docs.title: Ownership
docs.text:
• Reassign inactive owners
  • Require owner training

docs.title: Semantic
docs.text:
• Correct status/classification
  • Update documentation

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
• Patch webhook handler
  • Patch workflow logic

docs.title: Step 4 - Verify Containment
docs.text:
• Drift cluster dissolves
  • SLA pressure decreases
  • Dashboard stabilizes

docs.title: Step 5 - Prevent Recurrence
docs.text:
• Update schema
  • Update CI validator
  • Update workflows
  • Update governance charter if needed

docs.title: Canonical Visualization of Drift Severity, Frequency, and Owner Distribution
docs.text: This specification defines the structure, color scale, and data sources for the metadata drift heatmap.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To provide a high-resolution view of drift distribution across owners and severity levels.

docs.title: X-Axis - Owners
docs.text: Sorted by: (1) Critical drift count, (2) SLA breaches, (3) Reliability score ascending

docs.title: Y-Axis - Severity Levels
docs.text:
• critical
  • high
  • medium
  • low

docs.title: 3. Cell Value Formula
docs.text:
CellValue = DriftCount * SeverityWeight

Where: critical = 4, high = 3, medium = 2, low = 1

docs.title: 4. Color Scale
docs.text:
Value Color 0 #E8F5E9 (light green) 1-5 #C8E6C9 (green) 6-10 #FFF9C4 (yellow) 11-20 #FFE082 (orange) >20 #FF8A80 (red)

Override: Any SLA breach -> #D32F2F (deep red) regardless of score.

docs.title: 5. Data Sources
docs.text:
• drift_events table
  • sla_timers table
  • owner_reliability_snapshots
  • Dashboard API (/api/dashboard)

docs.title: 6. Update Frequency
docs.text:
• Drift events: real-time
  • SLA overrides: real-time
  • Heatmap recompute: every 5 minutes

docs.title: 7. Interactions
docs.text:
• Hover: show drift items + SLA timers
  • Click: open owner detail panel
  • Filters: severity, appendix, time range

docs.title: 8. Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
• Deterministic color mapping
  • SLA overrides always applied
  • Owner sorting reproducible

docs.title: Visualizing How Drift Spreads Across the Corpus
docs.text: This heatmap models how metadata drift propagates across appendices, owners, and drift types based on structural, referential, and automation coupling.

docs.title: X-Axis - Appendices
docs.text: Sorted by: (1) Drift density, (2) Schema divergence, (3) Owner reliability

docs.title: Y-Axis - Propagation Factors
docs.text:
• Structural coupling
  • Referential coupling
  • Ownership coupling
  • Automation coupling
  • Schema divergence
  • SLA stress

docs.title: 2. Cell Value Formula
docs.text:
PropagationRisk = FactorWeight * NormalizedValue

Where: - Structural coupling = 4 - Referential coupling = 3 - Ownership coupling = 2 - Automation coupling = 3 - Schema divergence = 4 - SLA stress = 5

docs.title: 3. Color Scale
docs.text:
Score Color 0-4 #E8F5E9 (light green) 5-9 #C8E6C9 (green) 10-14 #FFF9C4 (yellow) 15-20 #FFE082 (orange) >20 #FF8A80 (red)

Override: Any appendix with >= 2 systemic drift clusters -> #D32F2F (deep red).

docs.title: 4. Data Sources
docs.text:
• Drift clustering engine
  • drift_events
  • sla_timers
  • owner_reliability_snapshots
  • Schema version map
  • Automation stability metrics

docs.title: 5. Update Frequency
docs.text:
• Propagation factors: hourly
  • SLA stress: real-time
  • Schema divergence: daily

docs.title: Modeling How Drift Spreads Across Documents, Owners, and Appendices
docs.text: This model predicts how metadata drift propagates through the corpus based on structural, referential, and systemic dependencies.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To understand and forecast how drift spreads, enabling proactive governance intervention.

docs.title: A. Structural Coupling (SC)
docs.text: Degree to which documents share schema-dependent fields.

docs.title: B. Referential Coupling (RC)
docs.text: Degree to which documents reference each other.

docs.title: C. Ownership Coupling (OC)
docs.text: Degree to which the same owner maintains multiple documents.

docs.title: D. Automation Coupling (AC)
docs.text: Degree to which CI, workflows, and webhook logic affect multiple documents.

docs.title: 3. Propagation Probability Formula
docs.text:
P_propagation = 1 - (1 - SC)(1 - RC)(1 - OC)(1 - AC)

Where each factor is normalized 0-1.

docs.title: Stage 1 - Local Drift
docs.text:
• Single document
  • Single owner
  • No cross-appendix impact

docs.title: Stage 2 - Cluster Drift
docs.text:
• Multiple documents
  • Same appendix
  • Same owner or same schema version

docs.title: Stage 3 - Systemic Drift
docs.text:
• Multiple appendices
  • Multiple owners
  • Shared CI error signature

docs.title: Stage 4 - Corpus-Wide Drift
docs.text:
• Schema divergence
  • Automation drift
  • SLA system stress

docs.title: Local
docs.text: • Owner remediation

docs.title: Cluster
docs.text:
• Schema review
  • CI validator update

docs.title: Systemic
docs.text:
• Workflow update
  • Owner reassignment
  • Governance intervention

docs.title: Corpus-Wide
docs.text:
• Freeze merges
  • Full runtime audit
  • Executive notification

docs.title: Composite Score for Metadata Stability, Drift Resistance, and Governance Robustness
docs.text: DG-RI measures how resilient metadata is to drift, schema changes, and automation instability.

docs.title: B. Drift Frequency (DF)
docs.text: Drift events per 30 days.

docs.title: C. Drift Severity Index (DSI)
docs.text: Weighted severity: DSI = 4C + 3H + 2M + 1L

docs.title: D. Schema Divergence (SDR)
docs.text: Percentage of documents not aligned with current schema.

docs.title: E. Automation Stability (AS)
docs.text: CI + webhook + workflow uptime.

docs.title: F. Owner Reliability (OR)
docs.text: 30-day reliability trend.

docs.title: 2. Normalization
docs.text: DF_n = max(0, 1 - DF/10) DSI_n = max(0, 1 - DSI/20) SDR_n = 1 - SDR AS_n = AS OR_n = OR/100

docs.title: 4. Interpretation
docs.text: Score Meaning 90-100 Highly drift-resilient 75-89 Strong resilience 60-74 Moderate resilience 40-59 At risk <40 Drift-prone

docs.title: 5. Use Cases
docs.text:
• Drift forecasting
  • Owner coaching
  • Schema evolution planning
  • Automation tuning
  • Quarterly governance review

docs.title: Probabilistic Modeling of Drift, SLA Breaches, and Governance Stability
docs.text: This simulation uses Monte-Carlo methods to estimate governance outcomes under uncertainty.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To quantify: SLA breach probability, systemic drift probability, automation failure impact, reliability score degradation, and governance stress thresholds.

docs.title: A. Drift Injection Rate (DIR)
docs.text: Distribution: Poisson(lambda)

docs.title: B. Drift Severity Mix (DSM)
docs.text: Distribution: Multinomial(p_critical, p_high, p_medium, p_low)

docs.title: C. Owner Responsiveness (OR)
docs.text: Distribution: Beta(alpha, beta)

docs.title: D. Automation Stability (AS)
docs.text: Distribution: Bernoulli(p_success)

docs.title: E. Schema Strictness (SS)
docs.text: Distribution: Uniform(0.7-1.0)

docs.title: Step 1 - Generate Drift Events
docs.text: N = Poisson(DIR)

docs.title: Step 2 - Assign Severity
docs.text: Severity ~ Multinomial(DSM)

docs.title: Step 3 - Simulate Owner Acknowledgment
docs.text: Ack ~ Bernoulli(OR)

docs.title: Step 4 - Simulate Automation Behavior
docs.text:
• CI may misclassify
  • Webhook may drop events
  • Workflow may fail

docs.title: Step 5 - Update SLA Timers
docs.text: Simulated SLA engine increments timers.

docs.title: Step 6 - Compute Governance Stress Index (GSI)
docs.text: GSI = DriftLoad + SLAStress + AutomationPenalty

docs.title: 4. Outputs
docs.text:
• SLA breach probability
  • Systemic drift probability
  • Reliability score distribution
  • Automation failure distribution
  • Governance stress curve

docs.title: 5. Use Cases
docs.text:
• Schema evolution testing
  • CI validator updates
  • Workflow redesign
  • Governance risk forecasting

docs.title: Visualizing Reliability Across Owners, Appendices, and Drift Indicators
docs.text: This heatmap highlights reliability strengths and weaknesses across the metadata governance ecosystem.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To provide a visual, data-driven view of where metadata reliability risks are concentrated by owner and indicator, enabling targeted governance intervention.

docs.title: X-Axis: Owners
docs.text:
Sorted by:

  1. Reliability score (ascending — lowest reliability leftmost)
  2. SLA breaches (descending — most breaches left)
  3. Drift velocity (descending — fastest drift left)

docs.title: Y-Axis: Reliability Indicators
docs.text:
• Metadata quality score
  • Drift frequency
  • Drift severity index
  • Schema divergence
  • Automation stability
  • Owner reliability trend

docs.title: 3. Cell Value Formula
docs.text:
Each cell is scored as:

CellValue = IndicatorValue x RiskWeight

Indicator weights:

Indicator Weight Drift frequency 4 Drift severity index 4 Schema divergence 3 Metadata quality score 2 Automation stability 2 Owner reliability trend 5

docs.title: 4. Color Scale
docs.text:
Cell Score Color Meaning 0-4 #E8F5E9 (light green) Healthy 5-9 #C8E6C9 (green) Acceptable 10-14 #FFF9C4 (yellow) Caution 15-20 #FFE082 (orange) At risk >20 #FF8A80 (red) Critical

Override rule: Any cell where the owner has an active SLA breach is rendered #D32F2F (deep red) regardless of computed score.

docs.title: 5. Data Sources
docs.text:
• owner_reliability_snapshots: 30-day rolling owner reliability scores
  • drift_events: All drift events with severity classification
  • sla_timers: Active and resolved SLA timers per owner
  • Schema version map: Current and expected schema versions per document
  • Automation stability metrics: CI, webhook, and workflow uptime

docs.title: 6. Update Frequency
docs.text:
• Drift events: real-time
  • SLA timers: real-time
  • Owner reliability snapshots: daily
  • Schema version map: on schema change
  • Automation stability: every 5 minutes

docs.title: Composite Score for Metadata Stability, Drift Resistance, and Governance Health
docs.text: MG-RI measures the reliability of metadata governance across the corpus.

docs.title: B. Drift Frequency (DF)
docs.text: Drift events per 30 days.

docs.title: C. Drift Severity Index (DSI)
docs.text: Weighted severity: DSI = 4C + 3H + 2M + 1L

docs.title: D. SLA Compliance (SLA)
docs.text: 1 = no breaches, 0.5 = at-risk, 0 = breached

docs.title: E. Automation Stability (AS)
docs.text: CI + webhook + workflow uptime.

docs.title: 2. Normalization
docs.text: DF_n = max(0, 1 - DF/10) DSI_n = max(0, 1 - DSI/20) AS_n = AS MQS_n = MQS/100

docs.title: 4. Interpretation
docs.text: Score Meaning 90-100 Excellent governance reliability 75-89 Strong, stable governance 60-74 Moderate, needs improvement 40-59 At risk <40 Critical governance concern

• Quarterly governance review
  • Systemic drift forecasting

docs.title: Adaptive Optimization of Metadata Quality, Drift Reduction, and SLA Compliance
docs.text: This RL model learns optimal governance actions by interacting with simulated metadata environments.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To autonomously improve: metadata quality, drift reduction, SLA compliance, automation stability, and governance efficiency.

docs.title: A. State Space (S)
docs.text: S = [MQS, DF, DSI, SLA, AS, SDR]

docs.title: B. Action Space (A)
docs.text:
• Update schema
  • Update CI validator
  • Update workflow
  • Reassign owner
  • Trigger remediation
  • Trigger systemic drift review
  • Freeze merges

docs.title: C. Reward Function (R)
docs.text:
R = +MQS - DF - DSI - SLA_penalty + AS - SDR

Where: SLA_penalty = 10 for breach, SDR = schema divergence

docs.title: D. Transition Function (T)
docs.text: Simulated via metadata governance simulation engine.

docs.title: 3. Training Loop
docs.text: Step 1 - Observe state Step 2 - Select action (policy pi) Step 3 - Apply action in simulation Step 4 - Receive reward Step 5 - Update policy

docs.title: 4. Outputs
docs.text:
• Optimal governance policy
  • Recommended interventions
  • Predicted drift reduction
  • Predicted SLA improvement

docs.title: 5. Use Cases
docs.text:
• Schema evolution planning
  • CI validator tuning
  • Workflow optimization
  • Governance automation roadmap

docs.title: Simulation Framework for Drift, SLA Stress, and Governance Runtime Behavior
docs.text: This model simulates governance behavior under varying drift loads, SLA pressure, and automation stability.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To test governance resilience, predict failure points, and validate schema/automation changes before deployment.

docs.title: A. Drift Injection Rate (DIR)
docs.text: Number of synthetic drift events per hour.

docs.title: B. Drift Severity Mix (DSM)
docs.text: Percentage distribution of critical/high/medium/low drift.

docs.title: C. Owner Responsiveness (OR)
docs.text: Probability of acknowledging drift within SLA.

docs.title: D. Automation Stability (AS)
docs.text: Probability of CI/webhook/workflow success.

docs.title: E. Schema Strictness (SS)
docs.text: Strict vs. permissive validation rules.

docs.title: Step 1 - Generate Drift Events
docs.text: Events = DIR * Time

docs.title: Step 2 - Assign Severity
docs.text: Weighted random assignment using DSM.

docs.title: Step 3 - Apply Owner Responsiveness
docs.text: Ack = Bernoulli(OR)

docs.title: Step 4 - Apply Automation Stability
docs.text:
• CI may misclassify
  • Webhook may drop events
  • Workflow may fail

docs.title: Step 5 - Update SLA Timers
docs.text: Simulated SLA engine increments timers.

docs.title: Step 6 - Compute Governance Stress Index (GSI)
docs.text: GSI = DriftLoad + SLAStress + AutomationPenalty

docs.title: 4. Outputs
docs.text:
• SLA breach probability
  • Systemic drift probability
  • Automation failure probability
  • Governance stress index
  • Reliability score forecast

docs.title: 5. Use Cases
docs.text:
• Testing schema changes
  • Testing CI validator updates
  • Stress-testing governance runtime
  • Predicting systemic drift

docs.title: Governance Notifications -- Webhook Payload Schema
docs.text: This document defines the JSON payload structure sent to Slack/Teams when governance events occur. For the machine-readable JSON Schema see docs/governance/dashboard-api-schema.md.

docs.title: 1. Common Envelope
docs.text:
All events share this envelope structure:

{
  "event_type": "<type>",
  "timestamp": "<ISO-8601>",
  "environment": "prod | staging",
  "source": "uiao-governance-dashboard",
  "payload": { ... }
}

docs.title: 2. Event: drift_detected
docs.text:
Fired when CI or weekly workflow detects new metadata drift.

Payload fields: - document_id -- canonical document ID - title -- document title - owner -- owner handle - severity -- one of: low, medium, critical - issues -- array of issue objects with: type, field, description, detected_by, report_id - links -- object with: document_url, drift_issue_url, remediation_pr_url

docs.title: 3. Event: weekly_report_published
docs.text:
Fired when the weekly drift workflow completes and creates a GitHub issue.

Payload fields: - report_id -- weekly report ID (e.g., weekly-2024-12-01) - summary -- object with: total_items, critical, high, resolved_this_week, carried_over - links -- object with: weekly_issue_url, dashboard_url

docs.title: 4. Event: sla_breach
docs.text:
Fired when acknowledgment SLA (72h) or remediation SLA (14d) is exceeded.

Payload fields: - sla_type -- one of: acknowledgment, remediation - threshold_hours -- SLA threshold (72 or 336) - document_id -- canonical document ID - title -- document title - owner -- owner handle - age_hours -- actual age in hours - severity -- drift severity - links -- object with: document_url, drift_issue_url, owner_view_url - escalation -- object with: to (governance team handle), reason

docs.title: 5. Delivery Requirements
docs.text:
• Payloads must be delivered within 60 seconds of event occurrence.
  • Failed deliveries must be retried with exponential backoff (3 attempts max).
  • Webhook secret must be rotated quarterly.
  • All event types must be logged to the governance database.

docs.title: UIAO Governance -- Owner Reliability Scoring Algorithm
docs.text: This algorithm produces a single numeric score (0-100) representing an owner's governance reliability. It is the authoritative formula for the dashboard heatmap and quarterly governance reviews.

docs.title: 1. Input Variables
docs.text: Variable Definition TTA Average Time-to-Acknowledge (hours) TTR Average Time-to-Remediate (days) B Number of SLA breaches in last 90 days R Number of repeated CI failures W Number of weekly reports with unacknowledged items

docs.title: 2. Normalization
docs.text:
Each variable is normalized to a 0.0-1.0 range (1.0 = perfect, 0.0 = worst):

  • TTA_n = max(0, 1 - TTA / 72)
  • TTR_n = max(0, 1 - TTR / 14)
  • B_n = max(0, 1 - B / 5)
  • R_n = max(0, 1 - R / 10)
  • W_n = max(0, 1 - W / 6)

docs.title: 3. Weighted Score Formula
docs.text: Score = 100 x (0.30 x TTA_n + 0.30 x TTR_n + 0.20 x B_n + 0.10 x R_n + 0.10 x W_n)

docs.title: 4. Score Interpretation
docs.text: Score Meaning 90-100 Exemplary governance reliability 75-89 Strong, consistent performance 60-74 Acceptable but needs improvement 40-59 At-risk owner < 40 Governance intervention required

docs.title: 5. Usage
docs.text:
• Dashboard heatmap sorts owners by score (ascending = most at-risk first).
  • Quarterly governance reviews use score trends to identify systemic issues.
  • Scores below 40 trigger governance-review label on all open items.
  • Scores are snapshots -- recalculated weekly from the drift database.

docs.title: 6. Governance Reporting Metrics (Auto-Derived)
docs.text:
• TTA (Time to Acknowledge): Derived from needs-owner-ack -> acknowledgment timestamp.
  • TTR (Time to Remediate): Derived from issue creation -> PR merge timestamp.
  • SLA Breach Rate: Count of issues with sla-breached label per milestone.
  • Owner Reliability Score: Computed weekly; stored in owner_reliability_snapshots table.
  • Systemic Drift Index: Number of items entering governance-review per quarter.

docs.title: UIAO Governance Risk Early-Warning Scoring Model
Predictive Risk Scoring for Drift, SLA Breaches, and Systemic Failures
docs.text: This model provides a quantitative early-warning signal for governance risk across owners, appendices, and the entire corpus.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To detect governance risk before SLA breaches, systemic drift, or automation failures occur.

docs.title: A. Drift Velocity (DV)
docs.text: Rate of new drift items per week.

docs.title: B. Drift Severity Index (DSI)
docs.text:
Weighted severity score:

DSI = 4C + 3H + 2M + 1L

docs.title: C. SLA Pressure (SLAP)
docs.text: Percentage of items currently in sla-at-risk.

docs.title: D. Owner Reliability Trend (ORT)
docs.text: 30-day slope of reliability score.

docs.title: E. Automation Stability (AS)
docs.text: Workflow success rate, CI accuracy, webhook uptime.

docs.title: 3. Normalization
docs.text:
DV_n = min(1, DV / 10)

DSI_n = min(1, DSI / 20)

SLAP_n = SLAP

ORT_n = max(0, 1 - ORT/20)

AS_n = 1 - AS

docs.title: 5. Interpretation
docs.text: Score Meaning 0-29 Low risk 30-59 Moderate risk 60-79 High risk 80-100 Critical governance risk

docs.title: Moderate (30-59)
docs.text:
• Increase monitoring
  • Notify owners

docs.title: High (60-79)
docs.text:
• Governance steward review
  • Schema/CI evaluation

docs.title: Critical (80-100)
docs.text:
• Immediate governance intervention
  • Systemic drift investigation
  • Automation audit

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
The governance runtime is a three-tier system:

GitHub --> Webhook Handler --> Governance API --> Dashboard UI
                           --> PostgreSQL (state)

Each component has a deterministic role:

docs.title: A. Webhook Handler
docs.text:
• Receives GitHub events (issues, PRs, workflow runs)
  • Applies labels, SLA logic, and state transitions
  • Writes events + SLA timestamps to PostgreSQL
  • Triggers downstream notifications (Slack/Teams)

docs.title: B. Governance API
docs.text: Aggregates: drift reports, issue labels, PR metadata, SLA metrics, owner reliability scores. Serves the Dashboard JSON API contract. Provides a stable contract for the frontend.

docs.title: C. Dashboard
docs.text: Renders: corpus index, SLA heatmap, drift items, weekly reports, reliability scores. Calls API every 30-60 seconds.

docs.title: D. PostgreSQL
docs.text: Stores: drift events, SLA timestamps, owner reliability snapshots, weekly report summaries, governance interventions.

docs.title: 2. Directory Structure
docs.text:
governance-runtime/
|
|-- docker-compose.yml
|
|-- webhook/
|   |-- Dockerfile
|   '-- uiao-governance-webhook.js
|
|-- api/
|   |-- Dockerfile
|   '-- uiao-governance-api.js
|
'-- dashboard/
    |-- Dockerfile
    '-- src/
        '-- components/
            '-- GovernanceDashboard.tsx

docs.title: Drift Detected
docs.text: Webhook receives event -> Writes event to DB -> API exposes updated corpus index -> Dashboard updates within 5 minutes.

docs.title: SLA Timer
docs.text: Webhook cron job checks DB -> Adds sla-at-risk or sla-breached -> API exposes updated SLA metrics -> Dashboard heatmap updates.

docs.title: Remediation PR
docs.text: Webhook detects PR merge -> Marks issue as remediation-complete -> API updates corpus -> Dashboard marks item resolved.

docs.title: 4. Health Checks
docs.text: Component Endpoint Expected Response Webhook GET /health { "status": "ok" } API GET /api/health { "status": "ok" } Dashboard GET / Static HTML (200) Database pg_isready Responds on port 5432

docs.title: 5. Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
• Webhook receives GitHub events and writes to DB.
  • API returns valid JSON matching the dashboard schema.
  • Dashboard renders corpus index and SLA heatmap.
  • Drift -> SLA -> Remediation -> Resolution flows visible end-to-end.
  • All components pass health checks.

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime Chaos-Engineering Test Plan
Validating Drift Detection, SLA Enforcement, and Automation Resilience
docs.text: This plan defines controlled failure experiments to validate the resilience of the governance runtime.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To ensure the governance runtime remains stable, predictable, and drift-resistant under failure conditions.

docs.title: A. CI Validator Chaos
docs.text:
• Inject schema mismatches
  • Introduce false positives
  • Introduce false negatives

docs.title: B. Webhook Chaos
docs.text:
• Drop GitHub events
  • Delay event delivery
  • Duplicate events

docs.title: C. Workflow Chaos
docs.text:
• Force weekly drift workflow failure
  • Introduce long-running workflow steps

docs.title: D. Database Chaos
docs.text:
• Simulate write failures
  • Simulate stale reads
  • Simulate schema mismatch

docs.title: E. Dashboard Chaos
docs.text:
• Delay API responses
  • Return stale data
  • Break panel rendering

docs.title: Scenario 1 - Drift Detection Latency Spike
docs.text:
• Delay webhook events by 10 minutes
  • Expected: SLA timers remain correct; dashboard flags latency

docs.title: Scenario 2 - CI Misclassification
docs.text:
• Inject 5% false negatives
  • Expected: Systemic drift clustering detects anomaly

docs.title: Scenario 3 - Workflow Failure
docs.text:
• Disable weekly drift workflow
  • Expected: Dashboard freshness alerts; governance notified

docs.title: Scenario 4 - DB Write Failure
docs.text:
• Block writes for 60 seconds
  • Expected: API retries; SLA timers remain consistent

docs.title: Scenario 5 - Dashboard Staleness
docs.text:
• Freeze dashboard refresh
  • Expected: Freshness indicator turns red

docs.title: 4. Success Criteria
docs.text:
• Drift detection remains functional
  • SLA timers remain accurate
  • Dashboard reflects anomalies
  • Governance receives alerts
  • No silent failures

docs.title: Real-Time Monitoring of Runtime Stability Under Failure Conditions
docs.text: This dashboard visualizes how well the governance runtime withstands chaos events across CI, workflows, API, DB, and dashboard layers.

docs.title: A. Chaos Injection Status
docs.text:
• Active chaos tests
  • Failure types
  • Impact radius

docs.title: B. Drift Detection Resilience
docs.text:
• Drift latency under chaos
  • Drift accuracy under chaos
  • Drift loss probability

docs.title: C. SLA Timer Resilience
docs.text:
• SLA accuracy under chaos
  • SLA drift alerts
  • SLA cascade probability

docs.title: D. Automation Resilience
docs.text:
• CI validator resilience
  • Webhook resilience
  • Workflow resilience

docs.title: E. Schema Resilience
docs.text:
• Schema divergence under chaos
  • Deprecated field reappearance
  • Schema version fragmentation

docs.title: F. Resilience Score (0-100)
docs.text: Resilience = 100 - (ChaosImpact * SeverityWeight)

docs.title: 2. Update Frequency
docs.text:
• Chaos events: real-time
  • Drift metrics: real-time
  • SLA metrics: real-time
  • Automation metrics: every 5 minutes
  • Schema metrics: hourly

docs.title: 3. Governance Actions
docs.text:
• Patch automation
  • Update schema
  • Update CI validator
  • Update workflows
  • Trigger systemic drift review

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime -- Data Flow Reference
docs.text: This document defines how governance data moves between components. For component architecture see runtime-architecture.md.

docs.title: 1. Drift Detected
docs.text:
1. Weekly workflow or CI detects metadata drift.
  2. Webhook handler receives GitHub issue event.
  3. Handler writes drift event to drift_events table with severity + timestamp.
  4. Handler applies labels (metadata-drift, <severity>, needs-owner-ack).
  5. Handler creates SLA timer entry in sla_timers table.
  6. API aggregates updated corpus index.
  7. Dashboard displays updated state within 5 minutes.

docs.title: 2. SLA Timer Expiry
docs.text:
1. Webhook cron job (runs hourly) queries sla_timers table.
  2. For items with acknowledgment_due_at exceeded: adds sla-at-risk (48h) or sla-breached (72h) label.
  3. Updates sla_ack_breached flag in database.
  4. Fires sla_breach webhook event to Slack/Teams.
  5. Tags governance team on GitHub issue.
  6. API exposes updated SLA metrics.
  7. Dashboard heatmap highlights breached cells in deep red.

docs.title: 3. Remediation PR Opened
docs.text:
1. Owner opens PR linked to drift issue.
  2. Webhook detects pull_request.opened event.
  3. Handler adds remediation-in-progress label to issue.
  4. PR linked to issue in GitHub Projects.
  5. API moves item to Remediation In Progress state.
  6. Dashboard updates item status.

docs.title: 4. Remediation Complete
docs.text:
1. PR is merged.
  2. Webhook detects pull_request.closed with merged: true.
  3. Handler adds remediation-complete label.
  4. Updates remediated_at timestamp in sla_timers table.
  5. Weekly workflow confirms closure in next run.
  6. Dashboard marks item resolved.
  7. Owner reliability snapshot updated.

docs.title: 5. Weekly Report Published
docs.text:
1. Weekly workflow runs (Monday 09:00 UTC or workflow_dispatch).
  2. Drift scan script queries all markdown files for metadata issues.
  3. Report written to reports/metadata-drift-YYYY-MM-DD.json.
  4. GitHub issue created with weekly summary.
  5. Webhook fires weekly_report_published event.
  6. API updates weekly reports list.
  7. Dashboard weekly reports view refreshes.

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime Dependency Resilience Model
Quantifying Runtime Stability Across CI, Workflows, API, DB, and Dashboard
docs.text: This model evaluates the resilience of the governance runtime by scoring each dependency's ability to withstand failures.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To measure and improve: drift detection resilience, SLA timer accuracy, automation stability, dashboard correctness, and systemic-risk resistance.

docs.title: A. Redundancy (R)
docs.text: Ability to recover from failure.

docs.title: B. Fault Isolation (FI)
docs.text: Ability to contain failures.

docs.title: C. Recovery Time (RT)
docs.text: Time to restore functionality.

docs.title: D. Failure Impact (FI2)
docs.text: Severity of failure consequences.

docs.title: E. Observability Coverage (OC)
docs.text: Metrics, logs, and traces completeness.

docs.title: 3. Resilience Score Formula
docs.text:
Resilience = 100 * (0.25R + 0.20FI + 0.20OC + 0.20(1 - RT_n) + 0.15*(1 - FI2_n))

Where: RT_n = normalized recovery time, FI2_n = normalized failure impact

docs.title: 4. Component Scoring
docs.text: Component R FI RT FI2 OC CI Validator Medium Medium Medium High High Webhook Handler Low Low Medium High High Weekly Workflow Medium High Medium Medium Medium Governance API High Medium High Medium High PostgreSQL High High High Critical High Dashboard Medium Medium High Medium Medium

docs.title: 5. Governance Actions
docs.text:
• Increase redundancy
  • Improve observability
  • Reduce recovery time
  • Reduce failure impact
  • Strengthen fault isolation

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime -- Deployment Guide
docs.text: Operational deployment procedures for the governance runtime. For architecture see runtime-architecture.md. For health checks see runtime-healthchecks.md.

docs.title: 1. Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker 24+ and Docker Compose 2.20+
  • Node.js 20+ (for local CLI)
  • Access to WhalerMike/uiao-docs repository
  • GitHub App credentials (APP_ID, PRIVATE_KEY, INSTALLATION_TOKEN, WEBHOOK_SECRET)
  • PostgreSQL 15+ (included in docker-compose stack)

docs.title: 2. Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description GITHUB_APP_ID GitHub App ID GITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY PEM-encoded private key WEBHOOK_SECRET HMAC-SHA256 webhook secret GITHUB_INSTALLATION_TOKEN Installation access token GITHUB_TOKEN PAT for API access GITHUB_OWNER Repository owner (WhalerMike) GITHUB_REPO Repository name (uiao-docs) DATABASE_URL PostgreSQL connection string PORT Service port (defaults: webhook=3001, api=4000)

docs.title: 3. Local Deployment (docker-compose)
docs.text:
# Clone and configure
cd governance-runtime
cp .env.example .env
# Fill in all required env vars in .env

# Start stack
docker compose pull
docker compose build
docker compose up -d

# Verify
docker compose ps
curl http://localhost:3001/health
curl http://localhost:4000/api/health

docs.title: 4. Production Deployment (Helm)
docs.text:
# Create secrets
kubectl create secret generic uiao-secrets \
  --from-env-file=.env

# Install chart
helm install uiao-governance ./helm/uiao-governance \
  --set ingress.host=governance.example.com

# Verify
kubectl get pods -l app=uiao-governance
kubectl port-forward svc/uiao-governance 4000:4000
curl http://localhost:4000/api/health

docs.title: 5. Database Initialization
docs.text:
On first deployment, run the schema migration:

psql $DATABASE_URL -f db/schema.sql

docs.title: 6. Post-Deployment Validation
docs.text:
1. Verify all health endpoints respond (see runtime-healthchecks.md).
  2. Create a test drift issue with label metadata-drift.
  3. Confirm webhook applies needs-owner-ack label within 60 seconds.
  4. Confirm API returns updated corpus_index.
  5. Confirm dashboard reflects state change.

docs.title: 7. Rollback Procedure
docs.text:
# docker-compose
docker compose down
docker compose up -d --scale api=0
# Restore previous image tag in docker-compose.yml
docker compose up -d

# Helm
helm rollback uiao-governance

docs.title: 8. Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
• All containers pass health checks within 60 seconds of startup.
  • Webhook receives and processes test event within 60 seconds.
  • Database schema applied without errors.
  • Rollback procedure documented and tested quarterly.

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime Failure-Mode Dependency Graph

Failure Modes:

- CI misclassify     --> Drift undetected or false drift
- Workflow crash     --> Weekly drift missing
- DB write failure   --> Corrupted SLA/drift state

docs.title: UIAO Governance Runtime -- Health Checks
docs.text: Health check specifications for all governance runtime components. For architecture overview see runtime-architecture.md.

docs.title: 1. Component Health Endpoints
docs.text: Component Endpoint Method Expected Response Webhook Handler /health GET HTTP 200, body: {"status":"ok"} Governance API /api/health GET HTTP 200, body: {"status":"ok"} Dashboard / GET HTTP 200, static HTML Database pg_isready N/A Responds on port 5432

docs.title: 2. Health Check Cadence
docs.text:
• Internal health checks: every 30 seconds (docker-compose healthcheck interval).
  • External uptime monitoring: every 60 seconds.
  • CI health gate: run before any deployment.

docs.title: 3. Degraded State Definitions
docs.text: Condition Severity Response Webhook unhealthy Critical Alert governance team; halt event processing API unhealthy Critical Dashboard shows stale data banner Database unhealthy Critical All components degrade gracefully; queue events Dashboard unhealthy Low Governance ops shift to GitHub Issues view

docs.title: 4. Recovery Procedures
docs.text:
Webhook failure: Restart container. Check GITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY and WEBHOOK_SECRET env vars.

API failure: Restart container. Verify DATABASE_URL connectivity.

Database failure: Check disk space and pg_log. Restore from latest backup if data loss occurred.

Dashboard failure: Rebuild container from source. Check API_URL env var.

docs.title: 5. Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
• All health endpoints respond within 2 seconds.
  • Degraded state alerts fire within 5 minutes of failure.
  • Recovery procedures are documented and tested quarterly.

docs.title: Monitoring, Logging, Metrics, and Alerting for Governance Stability
docs.text: This plan defines how the governance runtime is monitored to ensure reliability, SLA accuracy, and drift detection integrity.

docs.title: 1. Observability Objectives
docs.text:

  • Ensure SLA timers remain accurate
  • Validate drift detection pipeline

docs.title: C. Traces

docs.text:

  • SLA timer drift > 1%
  • Webhook downtime > 5 minutes

docs.title: Visual Dashboard for Monitoring Runtime Stability and Chaos-Resilience
docs.text: This dashboard provides a visual overview of runtime resilience across CI, workflows, API, DB, and dashboard layers.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To give governance operators a single-pane view of how the runtime behaves under chaos conditions, surfacing resilience scores for each layer and triggering intervention when resilience degrades.

docs.title: 2. Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    subgraph ChaosInputs[Chaos Inputs]
        CIChaos[CI Chaos]
        WHChaos[Webhook Chaos]
        WFChaos[Workflow Chaos]
        APIChaos[API Chaos]
        DBChaos[DB Chaos]
        UIChaos[Dashboard Chaos]
    end

    CIChaos --> A[Drift Detection Resilience]
    WHChaos --> A
    WFChaos --> B[SLA Timer Resilience]
    APIChaos --> C[Automation Resilience]
    DBChaos --> D[Schema Resilience]
    UIChaos --> E[Dashboard Resilience]

    A --> F[Resilience Score]
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

docs.title: A. Chaos Injection Status
docs.text:
• Active chaos tests and their target layers
  • Failure types injected (latency, packet loss, process kill, disk full)
  • Impact radius (number of documents and owners affected)

docs.title: B. Drift Detection Resilience
docs.text:
• Drift detection latency under chaos conditions
  • Drift accuracy under chaos (percentage of true drift events detected)
  • Drift event loss probability during chaos injection

docs.title: C. SLA Timer Resilience
docs.text:
• SLA timer accuracy under chaos (timer drift in seconds)
  • SLA drift alerts triggered by chaos
  • SLA cascade probability score

docs.title: D. Automation Resilience
docs.text:
• CI validator pass rate under chaos
  • Webhook event delivery rate under chaos
  • Workflow success rate under chaos

docs.title: E. Schema Resilience
docs.text:
• Schema divergence rate under chaos
  • Deprecated field reappearance frequency
  • Schema version fragmentation index

docs.title: F. Dashboard Resilience
docs.text:
• Dashboard data freshness under chaos
  • Panel render failure rate
  • Alert delivery latency

docs.title: G. Composite Resilience Score
docs.text:
Resilience = 100 - (ChaosImpact x SeverityWeight)

Where SeverityWeight is highest for drift detection and SLA timer layers.

docs.title: 4. Update Frequency
docs.text: Metric Category Refresh Rate Chaos events Real-time Drift metrics Real-time SLA metrics Real-time Automation metrics Every 5 minutes Schema metrics Hourly Dashboard resilience Every 5 minutes

docs.title: 5. Governance Actions
docs.text: Resilience Signal Action Drift detection resilience < 80 Patch CI validator immediately SLA timer resilience < 90 Patch SLA timer service Automation resilience < 85 Patch webhook handler and workflows Schema resilience < 90 Force schema normalization sprint Composite score < 75 Trigger systemic resilience review

docs.title: Heatmap of Resilience Across Runtime Components and Resilience Factors
docs.text: This heatmap visualizes how resilient each runtime component is across key resilience dimensions.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To give governance operators a structured, color-coded view of resilience gaps across the runtime stack, enabling targeted hardening investments.

docs.title: X-Axis: Runtime Components
docs.text: Component Description CI validator GitHub Actions CI lint and schema validation Webhook handler GitHub webhook event ingestion service Weekly drift workflow Scheduled drift detection and reporting workflow Governance API REST API serving governance data PostgreSQL Governance data store Dashboard MkDocs-based governance documentation site

docs.title: Y-Axis: Resilience Factors
docs.text: Factor Symbol Description Redundancy R Number of redundant instances or replicas Fault isolation FI Degree to which failures are contained Recovery time RT Mean time to recover from failure (inverted: lower is better) Failure impact FI2 Blast radius of component failure (inverted: lower is better) Observability coverage OC Percentage of failure modes covered by monitoring Chaos-resilience CR Pass rate on chaos engineering test suite

docs.title: 3. Cell Value Formula
docs.text:
Each cell is a normalized score in [0, 1]:

ResilienceFactorScore = NormalizedFactorValue

For RT (recovery time) and FI2 (failure impact), the score is inverted:

Score = 1 - NormalizedValue

So that higher scores always mean better resilience across all factors.

docs.title: 4. Color Scale
docs.text: Score Range Color Meaning 0.0-0.2 #FF8A80 (red) Critical resilience gap 0.2-0.4 #FFE082 (orange) Significant gap 0.4-0.6 #FFF9C4 (yellow) Moderate gap 0.6-0.8 #C8E6C9 (green) Acceptable 0.8-1.0 #A5D6A7 (dark green) Strong resilience

docs.title: 5. Data Sources
docs.text:
• Chaos-engineering test results from runtime chaos test plan
  • Incident and recovery logs from governance API and CI systems
  • Observability coverage inventory (monitoring, alerting, tracing)
  • Runtime configuration documentation (redundancy, fault isolation design)

docs.title: 6. Update Frequency
docs.text: Data Source Refresh Rate Chaos test results After each test run Incident logs Real-time Observability coverage On infrastructure change Runtime configuration On deployment

docs.title: 7. Interactions
docs.text: Interaction Result Hover cell Show factor details and last chaos test result Click component Open component resilience report Filter environment Toggle dev, stage, prod views Filter time range Show resilience trend over selected period

docs.title: 8. Governance Actions
docs.text: Heatmap Signal Action CI validator row red Harden CI validator redundancy and fault isolation Webhook handler RT red Improve webhook handler recovery procedures PostgreSQL FI2 red Add read replicas and failover configuration Any component CR < 0.6 Schedule targeted chaos engineering sprint Full column red Escalate to governance board for infrastructure review

docs.title: Service Levels for Drift Detection, SLA Enforcement, and Dashboard Accuracy
docs.text: This specification defines the service level objectives (SLOs) and service level indicators (SLIs) for the governance runtime.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To ensure the governance runtime operates predictably, reliably, and with minimal drift or downtime.

docs.title: SLO 1 - Drift Detection Latency
docs.text: Objective: 95% of drift events detected within 5 minutes SLI: Time from GitHub event to DB entry

docs.title: SLO 2 - SLA Timer Accuracy
docs.text: Objective: 99% accuracy in SLA timestamps SLI: Difference between expected and actual SLA deadlines

docs.title: SLO 3 - Dashboard Freshness
docs.text: Objective: Dashboard data < 5 minutes old SLI: Timestamp of last API refresh

docs.title: SLO 4 - CI Validator Reliability
docs.text: Objective: 99% accuracy (no false positives/negatives) SLI: Validator error classification accuracy

docs.title: SLO 5 - Workflow Success Rate
docs.text: Objective: 98% success rate for weekly drift workflow SLI: Workflow run status

docs.title: SLO 6 - Webhook Handler Uptime
docs.text: Objective: 99.5% uptime SLI: Health check availability

docs.title: Drift Volume SLI
docs.text: Tracks weekly drift count.

docs.title: SLA Breach SLI
docs.text: Tracks number of SLA breaches per week.

docs.title: Automation Drift SLI
docs.text: Tracks automation failures (CI, webhook, dashboard).

docs.title: 4. Error Budgets
docs.text: SLO Error Budget Drift detection 5% of events may exceed 5 minutes SLA accuracy 1% timestamp deviation allowed Dashboard freshness 5 minutes stale allowed CI validator 1% misclassification allowed Workflow success 2% failures allowed Webhook uptime 0.5% downtime allowed

docs.title: 5. Governance Actions When Error Budgets Are Exceeded
docs.text:
• Trigger automation audit
  • Freeze schema changes
  • Increase monitoring
  • Patch CI/webhook/dashboard
  • Governance steward review

docs.title: Simulation Framework for Testing Schema Changes Before Deployment
docs.text: This simulator models the impact of schema changes on metadata correctness, drift generation, and automation behavior.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To validate schema changes safely by simulating metadata drift, CI validator behavior, workflow outcomes, dashboard accuracy, and owner remediation load.

docs.title: A. Proposed Schema Change
docs.text:
• Additive / Transitional / Breaking
  • Required fields
  • Deprecated fields
  • Field ordering rules

docs.title: B. Corpus Snapshot
docs.text:
• Metadata for all documents
  • Owner assignments
  • Schema versions

docs.title: C. Automation Rules
docs.text:
• CI validator logic
  • Webhook handler logic
  • Workflow logic

docs.title: Step 1 - Apply Schema Change
docs.text: Simulate schema rules against corpus snapshot.

docs.title: Step 2 - Detect Drift
docs.text: DriftCount = sum of Violations

docs.title: Step 3 - Simulate CI Behavior
docs.text:
• Validator warnings
  • Validator errors
  • False positives/negatives

docs.title: Step 4 - Simulate Workflow Behavior
docs.text:
• Drift workflow output
  • SLA timer updates

docs.title: Step 5 - Compute Remediation Load
docs.text: RemediationEffort = DriftCount * ComplexityFactor

docs.title: Step 6 - Compute Governance Stress Index
docs.text: GSI = DriftLoad + AutomationPenalty + SLAStress

docs.title: 4. Outputs
docs.text:
• Drift count
  • Drift severity distribution
  • Owner remediation load
  • CI validator error rate
  • Workflow success probability
  • Dashboard divergence
  • Governance stress index

docs.title: 5. Use Cases
docs.text:
• Schema evolution planning
  • CI validator updates
  • Workflow redesign
  • Governance risk forecasting

docs.title: UIAO Systemic Drift Clustering Algorithm Specification
Canonical Method for Detecting Structural Drift Patterns
docs.text: This algorithm identifies systemic drift by clustering drift events across documents, owners, and appendices.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To detect structural governance failures early by grouping drift events into meaningful clusters.

docs.title: 2. Inputs
docs.text:
• Drift events (severity, timestamp, document, appendix)
  • Drift type (structural, referential, ownership, semantic, automation)
  • Owner metadata
  • Schema version
  • CI validator error signatures

docs.title: 3. Feature Vector Construction
docs.text:
For each drift event, construct:

X = [severity_weight, drift_type_id, appendix_id, owner_id, schema_version, ci_error_signature_hash]

Severity weights: critical = 4, high = 3, medium = 2, low = 1

docs.title: Step 1 - Normalize Features
docs.text: Min-max scaling across all dimensions.

docs.title: Step 2 - Apply Density-Based Clustering
docs.text:
Use DBSCAN or HDBSCAN:

  • Detects arbitrary-shaped clusters
  • Handles noise
  • Identifies dense drift regions

docs.title: Step 3 - Cluster Classification
docs.text:
Clusters are labeled as:

  • Owner-centric (same owner, multiple docs)
  • Appendix-centric (same appendix)
  • Schema-centric (same schema version)
  • Error-centric (same CI signature)
  • Cross-appendix systemic (multiple appendices)

docs.title: 5. Systemic Drift Thresholds
docs.text:
A cluster is systemic if:

  • 5 or more drift events
  • Across 2 or more documents
  • Within 14 days or fewer
  • With 60% or higher shared feature similarity

docs.title: 6. Outputs
docs.text:
• Cluster ID
  • Drift type distribution
  • Affected owners
  • Affected appendices
  • Root cause hypothesis
  • Severity index

docs.title: 7. Governance Actions
docs.text:
• Schema review
  • CI validator update
  • Owner training
  • Workflow update
  • Governance intervention

docs.title: Canonical Actions for Detecting and Responding to Emerging Systemic Drift
docs.text: This playbook defines how governance stewards detect, validate, and respond to early signs of systemic drift.

docs.title: A. Drift Pattern Signals
docs.text:
• Same error signature across multiple documents
  • Drift clusters forming within 7-14 days
  • Cross-appendix drift correlation > 40%

docs.title: B. SLA Signals
docs.text:
• Multiple owners with sla-at-risk
  • SLA breach rate increasing week-over-week

docs.title: C. Automation Signals
docs.text:
• CI validator false negatives
  • Webhook event loss
  • Workflow failures

docs.title: D. Schema Signals
docs.text:
• Schema divergence > 5%
  • Deprecated fields reappearing

docs.title: Step 1 - Identify Pattern
docs.text:
• Review drift events
  • Compare CI signatures
  • Check appendix clustering

docs.title: Step 2 - Validate Signal
docs.text:
• Confirm via dashboard
  • Confirm via DB queries
  • Confirm via workflow logs

docs.title: Step 3 - Classify Risk Level
docs.text:
• Low: isolated pattern
  • Medium: multi-document
  • High: multi-appendix
  • Critical: corpus-wide

docs.title: Low
docs.text:
• Notify owners
  • Monitor for 7 days

docs.title: Medium
docs.text:
• Require owner remediation
  • Update CI validator rules

docs.title: High
docs.text:
• Governance steward intervention
  • Schema review
  • Workflow update

docs.title: Critical
docs.text:
• Freeze merges
  • Full systemic drift RCA
  • Automation audit
  • Executive notification

docs.title: 3. Prevention Measures
docs.text:
• Quarterly schema review
  • CI validator tuning
  • Owner training
  • Dashboard anomaly detection

docs.title: 4. Success Criteria
docs.text:
• Drift cluster dissolves
  • SLA pressure decreases
  • Automation stability restored
  • Reliability scores stabilize

docs.title: Automated Detection, Classification, and Scoring of Structural Governance Risk
docs.text: The Systemic-Risk Diagnostic Engine (SRDE) continuously evaluates the corpus for emerging systemic governance failures using multi-signal analysis.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To provide a deterministic, automated mechanism for: detecting systemic drift, classifying root causes, scoring systemic-risk severity, and triggering governance interventions.

docs.title: A. Drift Signals
docs.text:

  • Drift severity index

docs.title: B. SLA Signals

  • SLA-breached count
  • SLA trend slope

docs.title: C. Automation Signals
docs.text:

docs.title: D. Schema Signals
docs.text:
• Schema divergence
  • Deprecated field reappearance

docs.title: E. Reliability Signals

  • Appendix reliability trend

docs.title: Step 1 - Signal Ingestion
docs.text: Pulls from DB, CI logs, webhook logs, workflow logs, dashboard API.

docs.title: Step 2 - Feature Normalization
docs.text: X_n = (X - min(X)) / (max(X) - min(X))

docs.title: Step 4 - Classification

  • Cluster drift

docs.title: Real-Time, Multi-Signal Detection of Emerging Governance Failures
docs.text: The Systemic-Risk Early-Warning Engine (SREWE) continuously monitors the governance runtime for early indicators of systemic drift, SLA stress, schema divergence, and automation instability.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: To detect systemic governance failures before they escalate, enabling proactive intervention.

docs.title: A. Drift Signals

  • Drift severity index

docs.title: D. Schema Signals

  • Deprecated field reappearance

docs.title: Step 1 - Signal Aggregation
docs.text: Pulls from DB, CI logs, webhook logs, workflow logs, dashboard API.

docs.title: Step 2 - Feature Normalization
docs.text: X_n = (X - min(X)) / (max(X) - min(X))
